title: Trajectory plan: pricing gates & evaluation milestone approval — Skyline Data
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Li <evan@skylinedata.com>
Cc: Dana Kim <dana.kim@skylinedata.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (first draft)

Evan / Dana,

Thanks again for the time yesterday. As promised, attached is a first-pass Mutual Action Plan (PDF) that ties the evaluation milestones to commercial gates. High level:

- Week 0–2: Platform onboarding + smoke tests (no commit).
- Week 3–5: Pilot eval with 50k tokens/day — customer trial pricing (trial credits).
- Gate A (end week 5): If pilot passes acceptance criteria, customer commits to 3-month reserved capacity at 40% discount vs on-demand.
- Week 6–12: Ramp to target throughput and P95 latency SLOs; incremental discounts unlocked at 60% and 80% utilization checkpoints.

Key asks for you today: please confirm the acceptance criteria in section 2 (smoke/perf thresholds) and confirm the preferred billing entity for the contract.

I have also attached a pricing spreadsheet (iteration v1) but expect to iterate once we lock the checkpoint definitions.

Next step: if you’re comfortable, I can circulate an internal commercial note and a revised pricing iteration for approval by our sales finance and deals desk.

Best,
Aisha
Aisha Rahman | Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference
Attachments: 2026-01-15_SKY-line_MAP_v2.pdf (MAP, PDF); pricing_iteration_v3.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Evan Li <evan@skylinedata.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 11:04:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (first draft)

Aisha—

Thanks for the write-up and the draft MAP. Quick clarifications from our side:

- Acceptance criteria: does the pilot acceptance require both 95th pct latency < 200ms AND model quality delta within 3% vs our baseline? We need clarity for our internal signoff.
- Billing entity: procurement wants a 3rd-party PO to Redwood, not an AWS resale.
- Discount triggers: procurement prefers a single ramp discount at Gate A rather than multiple checkpoints to simplify approvals.

If you can send a revised pricing that shows a single step discount at the commit (and separates trial credits from reserved capacity pricing), we can route to procurement next week.

Evan
Evan Li | CTO, Skyline Data


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 16 Jan 2026 09:18:00 -08:00
Subject: Fwd: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (Skyline) — commercial ask

Marissa / Ben — forwarding Evan's note. They want: single-step discount at commit (Gate A) and clarified acceptance criteria. Proposed approach: keep MAP milestones as-is for engineering acceptance checks, but compress commercial discounts to a single committed-discount at Gate A with a small ramp bonus (one-time credit) if they hit 80% utilization by week 10.

Proposed numbers (quick):
- Pilot credits: $12k (one-time, applied during evaluation).
- Commit: 3-month reserved pool 40% off on-demand, minimum monthly $18k (commit).
- Ramp bonus: one-time $5k credit if utilization target reached by week 10.

 asks:
1) Marissa — can deals desk sign off on 40% + $5k ramp credit as a standard special? What approvals do we need?
2) Ben — are there any technical caveats for the acceptance criteria (latency vs quality gating) that will make the eval ambiguous?

I want to move a revised pricing packet + MAP (single discount variant) to procurement by Monday.

Thanks,
Aisha

--- Forwarded message ---
(Attachments: pricing_iteration_v3.xlsx)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 08:56:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (Skyline) — commercial ask

Aisha —

40% + $5k ramp credit is acceptable from a commercial standpoint for a mid-market deal with clear acceptance criteria, but we should:

- Add a defined expiration (30 days) on the offer to avoid open-ended approvals.
- Put the $5k as a usage credit applied in month 2 rather than an upfront rebate; that helps accounting.
- Require procurement to sign DocuSign that confirms billing entity and PO flow before we turn on reserved capacity.

I’ll pre-clear this with deals desk/finance — no material legal redlines expected on standard terms. If procurement pushes for additional SLAs, escalate to me before legal review.

Approve to proceed to a commercial revision and DocuSign.

Marissa
VP, Commercial
Redwood Inference


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Li <evan@skylinedata.com>
Cc: Dana Kim <dana.kim@skylinedata.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Skyline Data Procurement <procurement@skylinedata.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Jan 2026 14:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Revised MAP + single-step commercial offer (requested)

Evan / Dana —

Per our internal approvals, please find the attached updated MAP and commercial packet. Summary of changes since my first draft:

- Commercial: single-step commit discount at Gate A — 40% off on-demand for a 3-month reserved pool, $18k minimum monthly commitment.
- Pilot: $12k evaluation credits applied during the pilot window.
- Ramp bonus: $5k usage credit applied in month 2 if utilization >= 80% by week 10.
- Expiration: this offer is valid for 30 days from the date of this email.

Acceptance criteria (engineering):
1) P95 inference latency < 200ms measured on the target sequence mix.
2) Model quality delta within 3% of baseline on the evaluation prompt set.
3) No critical security or data residency blockers remain open.

Next steps for Skyline to progress:
- Confirm acceptance criteria and billing entity (reply OK).
- Procurement to execute DocuSign (attached).
- Once DocuSign is complete, Redwood will provision the reserved capacity and apply pilot credits.

Attachments: pricing_iteration_v3.xlsx; docusign-skyline-map.pdf

Please reply with confirmation or any final commercial asks. If you want a short joint call to walk procurement through DocuSign, I can schedule with Marissa and Ben this week.

Best,
Aisha
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Attachments: pricing_iteration_v3.xlsx (updated pricing, spreadsheet); docusign-skyline-map.pdf (DocuSign envelope)

title: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path
preview:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 8:12 AM
Subject: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Aisha,

Requesting a time-bound exception to ship an UNSIGNED Redwood Private offline hotfix bundle using the break-glass process.

Summary
- Customer impact: Helios Defense pilot environment is blocked on a high-severity regression introduced in Private 2026.01.0 (air-gapped install completes but runtime crashes on start due to an init container args parsing issue).
- Urgency: Their internal change window closes Fri 1/30. If we miss it, next window is ~3 weeks; pilot success criteria slips and exec escalation already started.

Why normal signing is blocked
- Our signing stage is currently hard-blocked due to HSM-backed key access failures in the release pipeline.
- Root issue is being tracked under INT-11048; infra has rotated the CI secret but the HSM policy still rejects the signing service token (403). We do not have a safe workaround that preserves key custody controls without bypassing the HSM entirely.

Proposed exception
- Ship an unsigned hotfix bundle (bundle build ID: rpvt-2026.01.0-hotfix.1+build.4127) directly to Helios Defense via Support/Solutions, limited distribution.
- Include manifest + checksums (SHA256) and require customer-side checksum verification prior to install.
- Follow up with a properly signed bundle as soon as signing is restored (target <48h).

Risk acceptance request
- We acknowledge this weakens provenance/integrity guarantees and increases supply chain risk if the bundle is tampered in transit.
- We will limit exposure by controlling distribution, using out-of-band checksum verification, and collecting explicit customer acknowledgment.

Asks
1) Security approval for the exception (break-glass) with any required conditions.
2) Guidance on required audit logging/evidence for compliance (what we must retain internally and what we must ask the customer to retain).

Attachment: EX-2026-01-28-01_exception_request_form.pdf

Thanks,
Connor O'Brien
Release Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 8:41 AM
Subject: Re: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Connor,

I can consider approving, but we need to be crisp on (a) scope, (b) compensating controls, (c) evidence.

Please reply with:
1) Exact distribution scope (who at Redwood will handle the bundle; who at Helios will receive it; how it will be transferred).
2) Concrete verification steps we require Helios to perform (commands + expected output). If you have an installer version dependency, call that out.
3) What gets logged locally by the installer when the break-glass path is used (event names, log locations). If this is not implemented in their current installer, say so.
4) Proposed time limit + rollback plan. When signing is restored, will we force the customer to re-install from a signed bundle or can we accept “signed next release”?

Marcus: please weigh in on whether this is acceptable under our exception policy and what minimum conditions are.

-Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
Security (Exec Sponsor)
Redwood Inference


From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 9:23 AM
Subject: Re: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Responding inline:

1) Distribution scope
- Internal handlers: Connor O’Brien (Release Eng), Colin O’Donnell (Solutions) as the delivery contact. No other internal forwarding.
- External recipients (Helios): two named platform ops contacts (will be listed on the distribution list doc).
- Transfer: Helios will pull the bundle from their existing secure file exchange with us (same channel used for prior offline bundles). We will not send the tarball over email.
- Distribution limitation: single customer only; no posting to general download locations.

2) Customer verification steps required
- We will provide a SHA256 checksum file for:
  a) the bundle tarball
  b) the included manifest file
  c) the runtime artifact tar(s)
  d) Helm chart tarballs
- Helios must run:
  - sha256sum -c hotfix-bundle-sha256.txt
  - and capture the output in their change record.
- Installer dependency: they are on installer v1.18.2. Break-glass install flag is supported; signature verification is normally enforced but in this case we will use the break-glass override to proceed with explicit warning.

3) Logging/audit events
- When break-glass is used, installer writes:
  - /var/log/redwood-private-installer/install.log (explicit “BREAK_GLASS_USED” marker)
  - /var/log/redwood-private-installer/audit.log (event: bundle_verification_override)
- This is present in v1.18.2.
- We will also require a support bundle capture post-install which includes the installer audit section (per our standard support bundle flow).

4) Time limit + follow-up
- Time-bound exception: 7 days max.
- Plan: Once signing is restored, we will issue a signed equivalent hotfix bundle for the same code change and request Helios to:
  - verify signature successfully in preflight
  - store the signed bundle alongside the unsigned one for audit continuity
  - we are NOT requiring immediate reinstall if their environment is stable, but we will require the signed artifact to be validated and retained.
- If signing is not restored within 48h, we will provide daily updates and re-request approval if distribution needs to expand (currently not planned).

Attachments:
- hotfix-bundle-sha256.txt
- distribution-list-helios-defense.txt

Connor


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 10:07 AM
Subject: Re: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Aisha,

Security Eng view: acceptable only as a narrow, time-bound exception with explicit customer acknowledgment and internal evidence.

Minimum conditions I recommend:
1) Dual control on the bundle build + checksum publication
- Connor + one additional approver (Neha or Vivek is fine) must attest the checksum file matches the tarball that was uploaded.
- Store the tarball hash + checksum file + approval email in our internal evidence folder (GRC can reference later).

2) Mandatory customer acknowledgment before install
- Helios must acknowledge in writing (email is sufficient) that:
  - bundle is unsigned due to a Redwood incident
  - they performed checksum verification
  - they understand the increased tampering risk
  - they will retain logs/change record outputs.

3) Fail-closed stays default
- Break-glass override should be used only for this specific bundle and must produce the override audit event.
- We should not recommend disabling verification globally.

4) Post-incident requirements
- Signed equivalent must be produced once pipeline is healthy.
- Internal postmortem: why HSM-backed signing became a release blocker without a safe fallback.

If those conditions are met, I’m OK with Aisha approving.

-Marcus
Marcus Lin
Security Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 11:02 AM
Subject: Re: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Adding Product context:
- This is a pilot-critical issue for Helios; missing the change window materially jeopardizes the pilot and the broader air-gapped launch plan.
- We are not asking to generalize the exception; single account only.

I support proceeding with Marcus’s conditions + Aisha’s evidence requirements.

Vivek
Vivek Kulkarni
Product, Redwood Private


From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 12:46 PM
Subject: Re: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Approved: EX-2026-01-28-01, with conditions.

Approval scope
- Single customer: Helios Defense only.
- Single bundle: rpvt-2026.01.0-hotfix.1+build.4127 only.
- Validity: 7 days from today. Any extension requires a new request.

Required conditions (must complete before customer installs)
1) Dual-control attestation
- Connor + Neha to reply-all confirming:
  a) tarball SHA256
  b) checksum file SHA256
  c) that the checksum file validates the tarball and included artifacts.

2) Customer acknowledgment
- Obtain written confirmation from Helios that they:
  - verified sha256sum output successfully
  - understand the bundle is unsigned and why
  - will retain verification output + installer audit logs in their change record.

3) Logging/evidence retention
- Internally retain:
  - this email thread
  - the uploaded tarball hash
  - hotfix-bundle-sha256.txt
  - distribution list
  - the customer acknowledgment
- Ensure the installer break-glass event is present in the support bundle collected post-install.

4) Follow-up
- Produce a signed equivalent bundle when the signing pipeline is restored and send to Helios with instructions to verify signature in preflight.
- Marcus to open/track postmortem action items for the signing outage as a release blocker.

Do not broaden distribution without coming back to me.

-Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
Security (Exec Sponsor)
Redwood Inference

- This is present in v1.18.2.
- We will also require a support bundle capture post-install which includes the installer audit section (per our standard support bundle flow).

4) Time limit + follow-up
- Time-bound exception: 7 days max.
- Plan: Once signing is restored, we will issue a signed equivalent hotfix bundle for the same code change and request Helios to:
  - verify signature successfully in preflight
  - store the signed bundle alongside the unsigned one for audit continuity
  - we are NOT requiring immediate reinstall if their environment is stable, but we will require the signed artifact to be validated and retained.
- If signing is not restored within 48h, we will provide daily updates and re-request approval if distribution needs to expand (currently not planned).

Attachments:
- hotfix-bundle-sha256.txt
- distribution-list-helios-defense.txt

Connor


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 28, 2026 at 10:07 AM
Subject: Re: Exception request (EX-2026-01-28-01): ship UNSIGNED Redwood Private hotfix bundle via break-glass path

Aisha,

Security Eng view: acceptable only as a narrow, time-bound exception with explicit customer acknowledgment and internal evidence.

Minimum conditions I recommend:
1) Dual control on the bundle build + checksum publication
- Connor + one additional approver (Neha or Vivek is fine) must attest the checksum file matches the tarball that was uploaded.
- Store the tarball hash + checksum file + approval email in our internal evidence folder (GRC can reference later).

2) Mandatory customer acknowledgment before install
- Helios must acknowledge in writing (email is sufficient) that:
  - bundle is unsigned due to a Redwood incident
  - they performed checksum verification
  - they understand the increased tampering risk
  - they will retain logs/change record outputs.

3) Fail-closed stays default
- Break-glass override should be used only for this specific bundle and must produce the override audit event.
- We should not recommend disabling verification globally.

4) Post-incident requirements
- Signed equivalent must be produced once pipeline is healthy.
- Internal postmortem: why HSM-backed signing became a release blocker without a safe fallback.

If those conditions are met, I’m OK with Aisha approving.

-Marcus
Marcus Lin
Security Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference

Adding Product context:
- This is a pilot-critical issue for Helios; missing the change window materially jeopardizes the pilot and the broader air-gapped launch plan.
- We are not asking to generalize the exception; single account only.

I support proceeding with Marcus’s conditions + Aisha’s evidence requirements.

Vivek

Product, Redwood Private

Approval scope

Required conditions (must complete before customer installs)

  c) that the checksum file validates the tarball and included artifacts.

2) Customer acknowledgment

Do not broaden distribution without coming back to me.

title: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: SendGrid Deliverability Support <deliverability@sendgrid.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 24, 2026 at 9:18 AM
Subject: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)

Hi SendGrid Deliverability team,

We rolled out a new onboarding email sequence this week and saw an unexpected spike in bounces + spam complaints. Can you help us diagnose whether this is reputation-related vs a configuration issue?

Context:
- Program: onboarding lifecycle emails for *new* Redwood Inference Hosted API signups (not a purchased list).
- ESP: SendGrid Marketing Campaigns + a small amount via API (transactional-ish “welcome/verify” message).
- Sending domain: mail.redwoodinference.com (From: “Redwood Inference” <welcome@mail.redwoodinference.com>)
- DKIM: enabled (SendGrid automated)
- SPF: includes sendgrid.net on redwoodinference.com
- DMARC: currently p=none (we can change; want to confirm recommended policy before we do)

Symptoms (last ~72 hrs):
- Increase in “Blocked” events in SendGrid UI
- Spam complaint rate briefly peaked above our internal guardrail (we target <0.08%; saw ~0.15–0.22% for a few hours)
- Bounce rate increased materially; mix seems to include “invalid mailbox” and “temporary deferral” (esp. Microsoft domains)

Volume change:
- We ramped from ~1–2k/day to ~10–12k/day (new signups + backfill to a subset that signed up but never activated, last 30 days).

Asks:
1) Are we seeing any domain/IP reputation degradation on your side?
2) Are there specific remediation steps you recommend (rate limiting, segmenting, suppressions, content changes) to get back to baseline quickly?
3) Do you see any authentication alignment issues that could be contributing?

Attaching a quick export from SendGrid stats and a Gmail Postmaster screenshot.

Thanks,
Ben

Ben Carter
Growth / Lifecycle Experiments
Redwood Inference

Attachment: sendgrid-stats-2026-01-20_to_2026-01-24.csv
Attachment: gmail-postmaster-screenshot.png

---

From: SendGrid Deliverability Support <deliverability@sendgrid.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 24, 2026 at 11:02 AM
Subject: Re: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the detail and the attachments. I’m looping in a Deliverability Specialist to review. In the meantime, a few quick clarifying questions that will help us pinpoint root cause:

1) Are you sending on a shared IP pool or dedicated IP(s)? If dedicated, when were they provisioned?
2) What % of this week’s volume is the “backfill” segment vs true new signups?
3) Can you share 2–3 recent Message IDs from blocked/complaint examples? (SendGrid Event Webhook payload IDs or Email Activity IDs work.)
4) Is the “welcome/verify” message sent from the same domain and same IP pool as the lifecycle sequence?

Based on what you shared, the combination of a sharp volume increase + backfill segment is often correlated with complaint and bounce spikes (stale addresses + recipients who don’t recall signing up).

We’ll respond with specific ramp/cleanup steps once we review your account details.

Regards,
SendGrid Support

---

From: Alicia Moreno <amoreno@sendgrid.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 24, 2026 at 1:26 PM
Subject: Re: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)

Hi Ben,

Alicia here from Deliverability Support.

High-level findings from the last few days of activity:
- Bounces: the majority of hard bounces are 5xx “mailbox unavailable / user unknown” (list hygiene issue), and a meaningful secondary bucket is 4xx deferrals from Microsoft (rate limiting / reputation signals).
- Complaints: complaint events are clustered in the backfill cohort. That cohort is also over-indexing on “not engaged” recipients (no opens/clicks).
- Authentication: DKIM is passing for most messages we sampled. SPF appears present, but DMARC alignment is inconsistent depending on which stream (Marketing Campaigns vs API) is sending. This is typically resolvable by ensuring the same authenticated domain is used across streams and that your DMARC policy is configured with alignment in mind.

Recommendations (prioritized):
1) Pause or sharply reduce the backfill segment for 48–72 hours.
   - If you need to keep it, reduce it to a small % of total daily send (e.g., 10–20%) and only include users with a recent site/session event.
2) Throttle volume ramp.
   - Instead of jumping to 10–12k/day, step up in increments (e.g., 2k → 4k → 6k → 8k) and keep each step for ~24 hours while monitoring complaint + deferral rates.
   - For Microsoft domains (outlook/hotmail/live), consider additional pacing (hourly sends) and keep spikes out of a single hour.
3) Suppress risky addresses.
   - Suppress role-based addresses (admin@, info@, support@) and any address that previously hard bounced.
   - Add a rule to suppress recipients who do not open any onboarding emails within the first 2 touches.
4) Align authentication across streams.
   - Use a single sending domain/subdomain consistently for Marketing Campaigns and API sends, or ensure both are fully authenticated and aligned.
   - Consider moving DMARC from p=none to p=quarantine once you confirm SPF + DKIM alignment; but do not change to enforcement until you validate alignment.
5) Content/UX:
   - Ensure the first email clearly states why they’re receiving it (“You signed up for Redwood Inference on <date>”) and has a prominent unsubscribe link.

If you can reply with:
- whether you are on shared vs dedicated IP
- approx. size of backfill segment that went out
- 2–3 example Email Activity IDs
…I can give more precise pacing recommendations.

Best,
Alicia Moreno
Deliverability Specialist
Twilio SendGrid

---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Moreno <amoreno@sendgrid.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 24, 2026 at 2:11 PM
Subject: Re: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)

Hi Alicia,

This is super helpful — thank you.

Answers:
- IP: shared pool (we haven’t moved to dedicated IP yet).
- Backfill: ~35–40% of total volume on Wed/Thu. It targeted “signup in last 30 days, no first successful request yet.” (In hindsight: a chunk of those were likely older/stale emails or folks who didn’t remember the signup.)
- Streams: welcome/verify is via API, onboarding sequence is Marketing Campaigns; both use welcome@mail.redwoodinference.com, but we may not have been consistent with reply-to (some are @redwoodinference.com).

Sample Email Activity IDs:
- 3Qp0xHf3S1yP5b1b8z (blocked/deferral)
- 9lK4mWc2R0uA1n7v3d (complaint)
- Q7z2tJm6V4rB8c9x1s (hard bounce)

Plan we can execute immediately:
- pause backfill for 72 hrs
- cap total to 4k/day and ramp +2k/day only if complaint <0.08% and deferrals stable
- add suppression for role-based + any prior hard bounces

Question on DMARC:
We currently have DMARC at p=none on redwoodinference.com. Would you recommend we set DMARC specifically on mail.redwoodinference.com or keep it at the apex? We want to tighten enforcement but not accidentally break the verify email.

Thanks again,
Ben

---

From: Alicia Moreno <amoreno@sendgrid.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 27, 2026 at 10:36 AM
Subject: Re: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)

Hi Ben,

Your plan looks right.

On DMARC:
- DMARC is evaluated on the RFC5322.From domain (the domain in the visible “From:” address). If your visible From is welcome@mail.redwoodinference.com, then DMARC policy is looked up for mail.redwoodinference.com (and organizational domain policy can apply depending on your DNS setup).
- In practice, keep DMARC policy at the organizational domain (redwoodinference.com) and ensure your subdomain inherits appropriately, OR publish an explicit DMARC record for mail.redwoodinference.com if you want independent control.

Given you have two sending streams, the key is alignment:
- Ensure DKIM d= is aligned with the visible From domain (mail.redwoodinference.com).
- Ensure the envelope/mail-from (return-path) domain is configured to align as well.

Suggested safe progression:
1) Keep p=none while validating alignment across Marketing Campaigns + API (verify messages included).
2) Move to p=quarantine at a low pct (if you’re using pct=) once alignment is clean and complaint rates settle.
3) Only consider p=reject after a stable period.

On the Microsoft deferrals:
- Hourly pacing helps. Avoid single-hour bursts.
- Monitor whether deferrals drop once complaint rate normalizes.

If you’re able to share results after the 72-hour pause + ramp, I can confirm whether you’re back in a “healthy” range from our view.

Best,
Alicia

---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Moreno <amoreno@sendgrid.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 27, 2026 at 3:42 PM
Subject: Re: Deliverability investigation: bounce + complaint spike on onboarding sends (Redwood Inference)

Hi Alicia,

Quick update after implementing the pause + pacing today:
- Backfill paused (only true new signups)
- Total volume ~3.8k
- Complaints down to ~0.06% today
- Microsoft deferrals still present but trending down vs Thu/Fri

We’re also coordinating with our internal infra team on DKIM/SPF/DMARC alignment and will keep DMARC at p=none until we confirm both streams are aligned (we have an internal ticket tracking it).

One more question: do you recommend we split “verify/welcome” into a separate subdomain/From identity vs keeping everything under welcome@mail.redwoodinference.com? Our goal is to protect the verification deliverability even if marketing-ish onboarding copy experiments fluctuate.

Thanks,
Ben

Ben Carter
Redwood Inference

title: Launch brief: Lifecycle growth experiments (onboarding email v2 + console nudges)
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 27, 2026 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Launch brief: Lifecycle growth experiments (onboarding email v2 + console nudges)

Team — consolidating launch details for the lifecycle growth experiments so we’re aligned on what’s going out, when, and how we’ll measure + escalate.

TL;DR
- Email onboarding v2: continuing A/B and expanding send ramp to broader new-signup cohort starting Wed.
- Console nudges: first experiment goes live behind feature flag to a targeted cohort (with holdout) starting Thu.
- Dashboards: activation funnel + TTFT are the source of truth for decisioning; we’ll use the same cohort defs across email + console.

What’s launching
1) Onboarding email v2 (A/B)
- Audience: new Hosted API signups (self-serve) with standard suppression rules (unsubscribed, bounces, internal domains).
- Segments (initial):
  A) New signup → no API key within 6h
  B) API key created → no first request within 24h
  C) First successful request → nudge to “first 100k tokens” milestone
- Variants: per PM-659; Soojin-approved copy only (no new copy changes during ramp).
- Guardrails:
  - Complaint rate: stop/hold if >0.08% rolling 24h
  - Hard bounce rate: stop/hold if >1.0% rolling 24h
  - Unsubscribe rate: investigate if >1.5% on any send
  - Support spikes: watch SUP-24871 tags (“didn’t receive onboarding email”)

2) Console onboarding nudges (experiment 1)
- Placement: Console home + API Keys page (only when user is eligible for the next step).
- Targeting: feature-flag cohort assignment with holdout group.
- Initial nudges we’re shipping:
  - “Create an API key” (if none exists)
  - “Make your first request” (if key exists, no request)
  - “Enable streaming” (after first request; only if SDK supports it)
- Dismissal: should persist for 14d (or until completion).
- Guardrails:
  - Nudge must never block key creation or navigation (see SUP-24905 learnings).

Timing / rollout plan
- Wed 1/28
  - Email ramp expansion: increase from limited cohort → 50% of eligible new signups (with holdout per PM-659).
  - Confirm deliverability monitoring cadence (Ben to own).
- Thu 1/29

TL;DR
- Email onboarding v2: continuing A/B and expanding send ramp to broader new-signup cohort starting Wed.
- Console nudges: first experiment goes live behind feature flag to a targeted cohort (with holdout) starting Thu.
- Dashboards: activation funnel + TTFT are the source of truth for decisioning; we’ll use the same cohort defs across email + console.

What’s launching

- Audience: new Hosted API signups (self-serve) with standard suppression rules (unsubscribed, bounces, internal domains).
- Segments (initial):

  B) API key created → no first request within 24h

- Guardrails:
  - Complaint rate: stop/hold if >0.08% rolling 24h

  - Support spikes: watch SUP-24871 tags (“didn’t receive onboarding email”)

-Nina

Ava

title: GA go/no-go: Safe apply + one-click rollback (optimization config) — approvals + checklist
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: 
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: GA go/no-go: Safe apply + one-click rollback (optimization config) — approvals + checklist

Team — aiming to make a go/no-go call tomorrow (Fri) for LIMITED GA enablement of the optimization config safe apply + one-click rollback feature flag (Hosted + Dedicated only; Private docs/installer shipped but flag remains customer-controlled).

Requesting explicit approvals from: Product (Marissa), Release Eng (Connor), Security (Aisha) to enable the feature flag for the initial allowlist.

Proposed rollout scope (LIMITED GA):
- Enable in prod for allowlisted tenants only (initial: internal dogfood + 3 design partners)
- Canary required by default for any apply that touches >1 region or changes quantization profile
- Manual rollback only (one-click rollback in Console/API; no auto-rollback for GA)

Go/No-Go checklist (current status as of 09:00 PT):

1) Core functionality
- [DONE] Versioned Optimization Config objects (immutable versions + metadata) (PR-19834, PR-19888)
- [DONE] Diff view + semantic validation (PR-19857)
- [DONE] Rollback API endpoint + authz behavior (PR-19902)
- [DONE] Canary rollout controller + pause/resume hooks (PR-19991)

2) Safety + correctness
- [DONE] Runtime apply transaction checkpoints (PR-19915)
- [DONE] Idempotent applier + retry safety (PR-19918)
- [DONE] Fix apply/rollback race leading to mixed worker state (PR-20111) — merged, needs final production verification
- [DONE] Gate hooks (5xx + p95/p99) (PR-19933) — default thresholds set; overrides RBAC’d

3) Observability + on-call readiness
- [DONE] Rollout Health dashboard panels + annotations (PR-20012)
- [DONE] Runbook drafted + gameday completed (Drive: rollback-gameday-plan; results summarized in checklist)
- [OPEN] SRE sign-off not required for this thread, but I have confirmation from on-call lead that runbook is “usable as-is” for limited GA

4) Security / compliance
- [DONE] Audit log events for create/validate/apply/promote/rollback (PR-19941)
- [DONE] RBAC policy bundle update for “optimization change operator” (PR-20070)
- [OPEN] Confirm audit event payload includes request_id + actor + scope + config_version + prior_version for rollback (Aisha: can you confirm this meets launch bar?)
- [NOTE] We had SUP-18544 (“missing rollback event”) previously — fixed in PR-19941; should be covered by e2e + manual validation

5) Console / UX
- [DONE] Console backend endpoints (PR-19952, PR-19960)
- [DONE] Confirmation modal includes blast radius summary + type-to-confirm for multi-region (per UX notes)
- [OPEN] Copy nit: modal uses “Revert” vs “Rollback” in one spot — low risk; can patch next week

6) Release readiness
- [OPEN] Release train sequencing: runtime + control plane + console already in prod; feature flag remains off. Need Connor to confirm no remaining backports needed.

Attachments:
- SafeApplyRollback_GA_GoNoGo_Checklist_v6.pdf
- RolloutHealthDashboard_Screenshots_2026-01-22.png

If approved, plan is:
- Fri 1/23 15:00 PT: enable for internal dogfood only (1 tenant)
- Mon 1/26: expand allowlist to 3 design partners (Hosted + Dedicated)
- Two-week observation window; then re-evaluate for broader GA

Please reply with: APPROVE / HOLD and any conditions.

— Ava


On Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:01 AM Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:01 AM PST
Subject: Re: GA go/no-go: Safe apply + one-click rollback (optimization config) — approvals + checklist

Release Eng view:
- Runtime/control-plane/console bits you listed are already on the Jan train and deployed to prod earlier this week.
- The only “must-have” for limited GA was the apply/rollback race fix (PR-20111) and its regression test — I see it merged + green.
- No pending backports for the flag enablement path.

Condition from my side:
- We gate Friday’s internal enablement behind a 30–60 min watch window with dashboard + synthetic load (canary job) running.
- If rollback is used, we capture timestamps + config_version in the release note for Monday.

I’m comfortable with LIMITED GA as proposed.

APPROVE (Release Eng) pending Security sign-off on audit payload.

— Connor
Release Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


On Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:26 AM Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:26 AM PST
Subject: Re: GA go/no-go: Safe apply + one-click rollback (optimization config) — approvals + checklist

Reviewed the audit event examples in the checklist + compared to our required fields.

Minimum required for launch (for enterprise auditability):
- actor (user_id + email) and actor_type
- action (apply/promote/rollback)
- request_id / correlation_id
- scope (tenant_id, model, region) + blast_radius classification if present
- from_version and to_version (rollback must include both)
- result (success/failure) + failure_reason
- timestamp in UTC

PR-19941 appears to include all of these. Please ensure:
1) rollback events include prior_active_version (not just “target_version”)
2) we’re not dropping events on retries (idempotency should still emit exactly-once externally; at minimum at-least-once with stable event_id)

If those two are true in prod, Security is OK with limited GA.

APPROVE with condition: Ava to confirm rollback audit payload in prod (one manual test on internal tenant) before enabling for external design partners.

— Aisha
CISO, Redwood Inference


On Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 2:08 PM Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 2:08 PM PST
Subject: Re: GA go/no-go: Safe apply + one-click rollback (optimization config) — approvals + checklist

Product perspective: we should ship limited GA now.

Rationale:
- We have multiple escalations where “mis-scoped config apply” or “regression after optimization change” caused avoidable downtime and escalations. One-click rollback + audit trail is the correct mitigation.
- The rollout constraints (allowlist + canary default + manual rollback only) are appropriate.

Conditions:
- Explicitly message “LIMITED GA” internally and to the three design partners (no promise of auto-rollback).
- Quantization changes: keep eval/quality gate REQUIRED unless the tenant is internal. (We can revisit in broader GA.)

APPROVE (Product) with the conditions above.

— Marissa
Director of Product
Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>

Proposed rollout scope (LIMITED GA):
- Enable in prod for allowlisted tenants only (initial: internal dogfood + 3 design partners)
- Canary required by default for any apply that touches >1 region or changes quantization profile
- Manual rollback only (one-click rollback in Console/API; no auto-rollback for GA)

Go/No-Go checklist (current status as of 09:00 PT):

1) Core functionality
- [DONE] Versioned Optimization Config objects (immutable versions + metadata) (PR-19834, PR-19888)
- [DONE] Diff view + semantic validation (PR-19857)
- [DONE] Rollback API endpoint + authz behavior (PR-19902)
- [DONE] Canary rollout controller + pause/resume hooks (PR-19991)

2) Safety + correctness
- [DONE] Runtime apply transaction checkpoints (PR-19915)
- [DONE] Idempotent applier + retry safety (PR-19918)
- [DONE] Fix apply/rollback race leading to mixed worker state (PR-20111) — merged, needs final production verification
- [DONE] Gate hooks (5xx + p95/p99) (PR-19933) — default thresholds set; overrides RBAC’d

3) Observability + on-call readiness
- [DONE] Rollout Health dashboard panels + annotations (PR-20012)

- [DONE] RBAC policy bundle update for “optimization change operator” (PR-20070)
- [OPEN] Confirm audit event payload includes request_id + actor + scope + config_version + prior_version for rollback (Aisha: can you confirm this meets launch bar?)
- [NOTE] We had SUP-18544 (“missing rollback event”) previously — fixed in PR-19941; should be covered by e2e + manual validation

5) Console / UX
- [DONE] Console backend endpoints (PR-19952, PR-19960)
- [DONE] Confirmation modal includes blast radius summary + type-to-confirm for multi-region (per UX notes)

title: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 9:08 AM PST
Subject: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Team — sharing a quick exec update on the serving-runtime tail latency workstream (KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction) since it’s now gating both SLO confidence and a couple of late-stage conversations.

Why this matters (business + technical):
- SLO / reliability: We’re seeing burst-driven p95/p99 spikes in sustained decode workloads with mixed sequence lengths, especially when request churn leaves the KV arena fragmented. In the worst case it creates opportunistic evictions at bad times and can tip us into OOM / request shedding.
- Cost: Today we’re compensating by leaving more headroom than we want (lower effective utilization). If we can keep KV memory contiguous/predictable under load, we should need less “safety padding” in dedicated pools and can be more aggressive on hosted concurrency without tail regressions.
- Customer pressure: This pattern shows up repeatedly in support and in enterprise eval questions (see SUP-28411, SUP-28433). PM has asked for a credible plan and crisp risk story.

What we’re doing:
- Allocator + fragmentation measurement: improve page/block layout + free-list management; add a fragmentation ratio metric that correlates with p99 spikes.
- Budgeted compaction: incremental compaction with an explicit time/bytes budget tied to scheduler boundaries (prefill/decode transitions) to avoid decode stalls.
- Tier-aware eviction: last-resort pressure relief that is safe + observable; interacts cleanly with pinned sessions.
- Guardrails: feature flag, kill-switch, backpressure integration, and rollout policy that auto-rolls back on tail regression.

Timeline (targeting runtime-1.22 train):
- Jan 20–31: discovery/measurement + final design/ADRs (in review now)
- Feb 3–21: implementation (allocator changes + scheduler hook + metrics + initial eviction policy)
- Feb 24–Mar 7: validation (benchmark lab + perf canary + stress/conformance)
- Mar 10+: staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → dedicated pools)

Risk management (what I want exec eyes on):
- Primary risk is introducing copy overhead / stalls during compaction. Mitigation is strict budgeting and scheduler-phase enforcement.
- Secondary risk is correctness edge cases (cancelled requests mid-relocation, pinned sessions). Mitigation is stress tests + conservative stopping conditions + kill-switch.
- We will hold rollout if: p99 up > agreed threshold, eviction rate spikes, or compaction time/bytes moved exceeds budget in steady state.

Asks:
1) Mateo/Sean: please confirm you’re aligned on “stop the line” thresholds + who has authority to flip the kill-switch during a canary.
2) Connor/Talia: I’d like this treated as a first-class item on the runtime-1.22 release readiness checklist.
3) Starting next week: weekly status note to this thread (wins, risks, current metrics, next gates). Keep it short.

Attachments:
- KV_Compaction_Review_Slides_v3.pdf
- Runtime_1.22_Rollout_Guardrails_Summary.docx

Ava Chen
VP, Product & Platform
Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Aligned.

A couple notes to help frame risk:
- We are not doing “stop-the-world” defragmentation. Compaction is incremental with a hard budget (bytes moved + wall time) and can no-op if we’re in a latency-sensitive phase.
- Eviction is a pressure valve, not the primary mechanism. We expect it to be near-zero in steady state; if eviction is non-trivial, that’s a signal we should treat as capacity/traffic policy rather than “normal.”
- Omar + Sofia are owning correctness + scheduler integration; Noah is doing architecture/correctness review on the invariants.

Re: timeline, I think Feb implementation window is realistic provided we keep scope to allocator + minimal copy/pack kernels.

Mateo Alvarez
VP Runtime
Redwood Inference


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

+1.

On the guardrails / authority question:
- During canary, oncall SRE has authority to flip the kill-switch if we hit rollback criteria (p99 sustained breach + compaction/eviction correlated) without waiting for runtime to be awake. Runtime gets paged/escalated in parallel.
- We’ll codify in the rollout policy: rollback on (a) p99 regression over baseline for N minutes, OR (b) compaction time budget violations + elevated tail, OR (c) eviction rate > threshold for N minutes.

Allison + Rafael are helping make sure the dashboards/alerts are in place before we expand beyond internal canary.

Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 12:41 PM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Thanks — that authority model works.

For the weekly status: please start Monday EOD (Jan 26) with a simple template:
- Current state: frag ratio trend + p95/p99 deltas (if available)
- Last week shipped: PRs landed / metrics added
- This week: next gate + owners
- Risks / asks (1–2 bullets)

Mateo, please have Omar post it (you can forward if needed). Sean, if anything changes on rollback criteria/alert thresholds during canary, call it out in the same update.

Ava

title: Dedicated capacity: GPU selection & commit options for CloudScale
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@cloudscale.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Dedicated capacity: GPU selection & commit options for CloudScale

Elena — thanks again for the call this morning. As promised, I’m sending a concise breakdown of Dedicated (reserved) pool options focused on the GPU tradeoffs you flagged (A100 vs H100 vs L40S), plus commit/throughput options and an initial price sketch. High level first, details below and attached (redwood_pricing_quote_v2.pdf).

Summary / recommendation
- If model family is transformer-based with long contexts (>= 8k tokens) and you need single-request latency: prefer H100 for hot serving of your largest models.
- A100 is the best performance/$ for steady-state batch and mid-context workloads; stronger memory configs available.
- L40S is the lowest-cost, good for inference when model is quantized and you can use sharding/multi-GPU for memory.
- Typical approach we’ve seen work well: baseline commit on A100s for predictable throughput + H100 burst capacity for tail-latency spikes and very large contexts.

Commit structures we can offer
- 12-mo reserved commit (recommended): lower base price, predictable throughput. Includes burst pool (up to +25% capacity) and autoscaling lanes.
- 24-mo commit: deeper discount if you’re comfortable with longer horizon.
- We can tier the commit into two SKUs (baseline pool + burst pool) so you only pay premium for H100 bursting.

Perf/Cost tradeoffs (rough guidance)
- H100: ~1.6–2.2x single-GPU perf on large context models vs A100 depending on kernel and sequence length; cost usually ~1.5–1.9x A100 on our pricing bands. Best when memory bandwidth and larger tensor cores matter.
- A100: excellent perf/$ across many LLMs; more memory SKU variants (40GB/80GB) helpful for larger model pins or fewer sharding ops.
- L40S: 30–50% lower cost than A100; works well for quantized 4-bit workflows or CPU-offloading flows, but requires benchmarking for accuracy/regression risk.

Throughput & guarantees

Cc: Elena Morales <elena.morales@cloudscale.com>
Date: Sun, 18 Jan 2026 11:18:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity: GPU selection & commit options for CloudScale

Ben / Laura / Elena — quick technical note and recommended sizing. TL;DR: baseline A100s + H100 burst; use 80GB A100s if you need single-GPU 75GB working memory. Details below.

Throughput ballparks (illustrative; exact depends on prompt shapes, batching, and model optimization):
- Short chat (~512 tokens): 16x A100s baseline: ~1300–1800 tokens/sec aggregate (roughly 80–110 tokens/sec per GPU with 512-token average; batching increases aggregate). 8x H100s: ~1400–2200 tokens/sec aggregate (higher peak due to tensor core speed).
- Mid-size (~2k context): 16x A100s: ~700–1000 tokens/sec aggregate; 8x H100s: ~900–1300 tokens/sec aggregate.
- Large (~8k context): 16x A100s: ~150–300 tokens/sec aggregate (memory & KV cache pressure); 8x H100s: ~300–450 tokens/sec aggregate.

Notes on memory and model placement
- 80GB A100 is the safe single-GPU option for ~75GB working sets. If you want to avoid cross-GPU sharding complexity, pick 80GB A100s.
- H100 has better FP16/ampere tensor core throughput; for some very large models you still need sharding but H100 reduces kernels and can reduce latency.
- L40S works well for quantized 4-bit where memory footprint drops; good for lower-cost replicas or for embedding/backfill workloads.

Perf/$ and pricing guidance (policy-level numbers to use in negotiation)
- Relative perf bands we observe: H100 ~1.6–2.2x vs A100 on large-context transformer inference; cost multiplier on list pricing typically ~1.5–1.9x. So on perf/$ H100 can be better for latency-critical large-context inference but worse for simple batch throughput.
- L40S is roughly 0.5–0.7x cost vs A100 but also 0.5–0.8x raw perf depending on quantization. Best for cheap replicas.

Routing & mixed pools
- Yes — Redwood supports multi-pool routing policies: route by model tag, latency SLO, or cost threshold. You can set route rules like: prefer A100 for requests geo=us and cost<=X, fall back to H100 when latency budget <Y or token length >Z.

Suggested pilot commit (for 200k MAU conversational pilot)
- Baseline: 12x A100-80GB (handles steady-state chat + mid-size models).
- Burst: 4x H100 attached as burst pool (auto-burst up to 30% for spikes; billed only when used).
- Optional: 8x L40S for background embedding workloads / async backfills.

Commercial levers to propose to procurement
- 12-month commit: 15% discount off list reserved rate for the mixed pool above.
- 24-month commit: 25% discount + 90-day free pilot credits for multi-region testing.
- We can structure the deal so baseline A100 cost is locked; H100 charged on a per-use burst basis with a lower committed discount band.

SLA & monitoring
- We can commit to allocation availability and an SLO for request routing; detailed tokens/sec guarantees require explicit workload profiling (we can run a short benchmarking window).

If you want, I can run a quick 48–72 hour benchmark using sample prompts to quantify exact tokens/sec and median p50/p95 latencies per pool. That benchmarking data will give procurement the numbers they need.

Summary / recommendation
- If model family is transformer-based with long contexts (>= 8k tokens) and you need single-request latency: prefer H100 for hot serving of your largest models.
- A100 is the best performance/$ for steady-state batch and mid-context workloads; stronger memory configs available.
- L40S is the lowest-cost, good for inference when model is quantized and you can use sharding/multi-GPU for memory.
- Typical approach we’ve seen work well: baseline commit on A100s for predictable throughput + H100 burst capacity for tail-latency spikes and very large contexts.

Commit structures we can offer
- 12-mo reserved commit (recommended): lower base price, predictable throughput. Includes burst pool (up to +25% capacity) and autoscaling lanes.
- 24-mo commit: deeper discount if you’re comfortable with longer horizon.
- We can tier the commit into two SKUs (baseline pool + burst pool) so you only pay premium for H100 bursting.

Perf/Cost tradeoffs (rough guidance)
- H100: ~1.6–2.2x single-GPU perf on large context models vs A100 depending on kernel and sequence length; cost usually ~1.5–1.9x A100 on our pricing bands. Best when memory bandwidth and larger tensor cores matter.
- A100: excellent perf/$ across many LLMs; more memory SKU variants (40GB/80GB) helpful for larger model pins or fewer sharding ops.
- L40S: 30–50% lower cost than A100; works well for quantized 4-bit workflows or CPU-offloading flows, but requires benchmarking for accuracy/regression risk.

Throughput & guarantees
- We offer per-commit throughput profiles (tokens/sec per pool) and an SLA for allocation (uptime + allocation fairness). From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 10:28:00 -0800
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Ben / team —

Proposed high-level runbook (draft):
1) Intake validation: confirm tenant-id, date window, PoA / signed intake.
2) Snapshot: create a read-only manifest of current artifacts (list + sizes + hashes).
3) Quiesce: disable new writes to tenant nm-stg-42 for the window of execution (short maintenance flag).
4) KV/Cache purge: remove per-tenant KV caches and ephemeral prefix caches (safe deletion using tombstones + verification).
5) Prompt/output sweep: run artifact deletions for prompt inputs and generated outputs in the object store with multi-pass verification.
6) Logchain pruning: redact or remove request traces from the runtime logging shard, preserving audit-level metadata only if required by the customer.
7) Manifest & attest: compile erase-manifest.csv, produce hashes, timestamps, and route DocuSign attestation for signature.
8) Post-check: run integrity checks and return system to normal state.

Impact notes: shared model weights and precomputed public embeddings would not be touched unless explicitly requested — call this out in final scope. If NovaMed confirms embeddings are to be removed, that changes the runbook (longer duration).

I'll prepare a formal runbook doc and an ops ticket; Priyom, can you confirm the expected elapsed time for steps 4-6 and whether we need a rolling-backup snapshot?

Lauren Bishop
Senior Customer Reliability Manager

From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 11:05:00 -0800
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Ben / Lauren —

Estimate: core sweep (KV purge + object deletes + logchain pruning) ~ 4-6 hours for nm-stg-42 if we run in parallel across three GPU-serving shards. We'll run multi-pass deletes and then a verification pass to compute hashes. I will create OpsTicket RW-12345 and link the runbook draft. We do not need a full backup snapshot because we will create the pre-run manifest which serves as the pre-state record; however, if NovaMed requires a 30-day legal hold prior to deletion, we'd delay execution.

I'll attach the runbook draft and link to the internal ops checklist.

Priyom

From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@novamed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Hi Ben,

We need Redwood's assistance to permanently remove prompt inputs, generated outputs and associated runtime logs tied to the NovaMed staging tenant (tenant-id: nm-stg-42) for the window Oct 1 2025 -> Dec 31 2025. This is part of our contractual data minimization step ahead of a regulatory audit.

Deliverables we need:
- An operational runbook describing steps you'll execute (what is deleted, what is retained, order of operations).
- A post-run proof package: erase manifest (file list + hashes), timestamps, and a signed attestation (DocuSign or equivalent).
- Expected completion ETA and a calendar window for the purge (we want no customer-visible downtime).

Please confirm whether logs stored for debugging (request traces, model KV cache snapshots, transient OTPs) are included in the erase scope and if any artifacts will remain for retention/forensics. We also need to know whether this purge will impact other NovaMed tenants or shared artifacts.

Can you get us a short runbook and a suggested execution window by end of week? We need to deadline this with our compliance team.

Thanks,
Jordan Lee
Head of Security Operations
NovaMed Systems
security@novamed.com

--
Attachment: nm-erasure-intake-form.pdf (stub)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@novamed.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Jordan —

Thanks for the clear scope. We can support a full sweep of prompt inputs, generated outputs, and per-request runtime logs scoped to tenant nm-stg-42 for the requested date window. A couple quick clarifying items before we draft the runbook:
- Please confirm whether the scope includes precomputed embeddings and archived model snapshots tied to the tenant (yes/no).
- Do you require a rolling retention-freeze for 30 days prior to run to allow for legal hold requests?

Tentatively we can schedule an execution window starting Tue 20 Jan 2026 (overnight UTC window) and provide the erase manifest + signed attestation within 24 hours of completion. I will loop in Ops and our Compliance lead and deliver a short runbook draft by Mon EOD.

I'll attach the intake form for signature and forward the request internally.

Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0119

--
Attachment: intake-form-novamed.pdf (stub)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 10:12:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Forwarding the request from NovaMed (below). Can Ops and Config run a safe play-by-play and estimate the outage risk / rollback plan? Compliance wants the signed attestation and erase manifest.

--- Forwarded message ---
From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@novamed.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com

[original message above]

Lauren — can you draft the runbook steps (high-level) and call out any platform-level dependencies (shared KV namespaces, retention snapshots). Priyom — can you estimate execution time and confirm we can produce a manifest with file hashes and timestamps?

Ben

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 10:28:00 -0800
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Ben / team —

Proposed high-level runbook (draft):
1) Intake validation: confirm tenant-id, date window, PoA / signed intake.
2) Snapshot: create a read-only manifest of current artifacts (list + sizes + hashes).
3) Quiesce: disable new writes to tenant nm-stg-42 for the window of execution (short maintenance flag).
4) KV/Cache purge: remove per-tenant KV caches and ephemeral prefix caches (safe deletion using tombstones + verification).
5) Prompt/output sweep: run artifact deletions for prompt inputs and generated outputs in the object store with multi-pass verification.
6) Logchain pruning: redact or remove request traces from the runtime logging shard, preserving audit-level metadata only if required by the customer.
7) Manifest & attest: compile erase-manifest.csv, produce hashes, timestamps, and route DocuSign attestation for signature.
8) Post-check: run integrity checks and return system to normal state.

Impact notes: shared model weights and precomputed public embeddings would not be touched unless explicitly requested — call this out in final scope. If NovaMed confirms embeddings are to be removed, that changes the runbook (longer duration).

I'll prepare a formal runbook doc and an ops ticket; Priyom, can you confirm the expected elapsed time for steps 4-6 and whether we need a rolling-backup snapshot?

Lauren Bishop
Senior Customer Reliability Manager

From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 11:05:00 -0800
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: purge of prompt outputs, prompt logs and runtime traces (NovaMed)

Ben / Lauren —

Estimate: core sweep (KV purge + object deletes + logchain pruning) ~ 4-6 hours for nm-stg-42 if we run in parallel across three GPU-serving shards. We'll run multi-pass deletes and then a verification pass to compute hashes. I will create OpsTicket RW-12345 and link the runbook draft. We do not need a full backup snapshot because we will create the pre-run manifest which serves as the pre-state record; however, if NovaMed requires a 30-day legal hold prior to deletion, we'd delay execution.

I'll attach the runbook draft and link to the internal ops checklist.

Priyom
Ops Engineering — Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@novamed.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 08:47:00 -0800
Subject: Operational runbook and scheduled window for NovaMed tenant nm-stg-42 purge

Jordan —

Per our internal review, attached is the execution summary and the runbook we propose for the purge of prompt inputs, generated outputs and runtime logs for tenant nm-stg-42 covering Oct 1 2025 -> Dec 31 2025. Key points:

Planned window: Tue 20 Jan 2026 02:00 - 08:00 UTC (overnight to minimize user impact).
Scope (final): prompt inputs, model-generated outputs, per-request runtime logs and ephemeral KV caches tied to nm-stg-42. Shared model weights, global precomputed embeddings and cross-tenant artifacts will NOT be deleted unless you explicitly request that.
Execution steps (summary):
- Intake validation & DocuSign approval (attached DocuSign link).
- Pre-run manifest generation (we will attach erase-manifest-20260120.csv after completion).
- Disable tenant writes and run parallel delete jobs across three serving shards.
- Run two verification passes and produce hash-signed manifest.
- Post-run integrity check and re-enable tenant.

Artifacts we will deliver within 24 hours after completion: erase-manifest-20260120.csv, novamed-runbook-v1.pdf, and a DocuSign-signed attestation.

Please sign the intake (attached) and the DocuSign attestation to confirm authorization for the scheduled window. If you need embeddings removed as well, reply with explicit confirmation; that will add ~6 hours.

Attachments: novamed-runbook-v1.pdf (stub); erase-manifest-template.csv (stub); DocuSign:0xA12B34

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0119

--
Related link: https://drive.redwood.com/ops/runbooks/novamed-sanitization-v1


From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@novamed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com
Date: Tue, 20 Jan 2026 15:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Operational runbook and scheduled window for NovaMed tenant nm-stg-42 purge

Ben / team —

Confirmed: we signed DocuSign attestation and authorized the execution window. Thank you for the clear runbook. We confirm the scope as stated (do NOT remove precomputed embeddings).

Per our compliance process, NovaMed accepts the erase-manifest and signed attestation as sufficient evidence. Please make sure the manifest includes file-level SHA256 hashes and server timestamps. Reply with final delivery link when available.

Appreciate the quick turnaround.

Jordan Lee
Head of Security Operations
NovaMed Systems
security@novamed.com

--
Attachment: docusign-erasure-attestation.pdf (stub)

title: MediWell HIPAA discovery: BAA availability, PHI boundaries, deployment options
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Dr. Susan Park <susan.park@mediwell.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: MediWell discovery follow-up — HIPAA, BAA, deployment modes (recap & asks)

Hi Susan — thanks for taking the call earlier. Quick recap from our side and a couple of asks so we can align the security and procurement streams: 

- Recap: demo covered inference options (hosted API, Dedicated reserved pools, Private VPC/on-prem) and runtime features relevant to PHI controls (KV/prefix caching controls, no-persistent-logging mode, customer KMS integrations).
- Questions we need answered to map an eligible hosting path: 
  1) Do you require a signed BAA for any PHI processing? If yes, will a standard Redwood BAA be acceptable or do you need MediWell legal to redline?
  2) What is the strict PHI boundary for this PoC — are we allowed to receive any direct identifiers in prompts, or will MediWell only send tokenized/de-identified data?
  3) Preferred deployment mode for initial testing: Hosted (with BAA + data handling controls), Dedicated reserved capacity, or Private VPC/on-prem? Any hard constraints on data residency (region/az)?
  4) Please share your vendor security questionnaire so our security team can start filling it out.

Attaching a short SOC 2 summary for context. If helpful, we can set up a dedicated security session next week with Karthik + our security lead. Proposed next step: Susan, can you send the questionnaire and confirm procurement contact? We’ll circulate a draft BAA for legal to review.

Thanks — Ben

--
Ben Carter
Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
Attachment: Redwood_SOC2_Summary_2025.pdf (PDF, 2 pages)


From: Dr. Susan Park <susan.park@mediwell.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jamie Torres <jamie.torres@mediwell.com>
Date: Fri, 16 Jan 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MediWell discovery follow-up — HIPAA, BAA, deployment modes (recap & asks)

Ben — thanks, good recap. A few answers and notes from our side: 

1) BAA: MediWell requires a signed BAA for any engagement that could process PHI. We typically review vendor BAAs, but if you can provide a standard Redwood draft that would speed things up.

2) PHI boundary: For the PoC our engineering team will only send de-identified clinical text; however, long-term we expect workflows that may include limited identifiers (MRN, partial SSN as token). Primary constraints: no persistent logging of raw prompts or generated output that contain PHI, and audit logs must be available for the customer.

3) Deployment preference: Procurement prefers Dedicated or Private VPC for anything that will touch identifiers. Hosted with a BAA is acceptable for de-identified workflows only. Data residency: must be US-only (east coast preferred).

4) Questionnaire: attaching our vendor security questionnaire — we'd like that back in two weeks if possible. Procurement contact is Jamie Torres (cc'd) — Jamie will handle contracting and SOW.

We’ll need a short call with your security representative to walk through the questionnaire and the SOC 2 evidence. Dates next week look flexible Tue/Wed mornings PST.

Thanks,
Susan

--
Dr. Susan Park
Head of Integrations, MediWell Health
susan.park@mediwell.com
Attachment: MediWell_Vendor_Sec_Questionnaire.xlsx (Excel, 38 rows)


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Dr. Susan Park <susan.park@mediwell.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 17 Jan 2026 11:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MediWell discovery follow-up — HIPAA, BAA, deployment modes (recap & asks)

Susan — great, really helpful. I pulled together the minimal options and next steps so your procurement and security teams can decide quickly: 

Option A — Hosted (fastest onboarding)- Requirements: Signed BAA + agreed non-persistent logging mode; we can enable US-only tenancy and disable inference persistence for the project.- Suitable for: de-identified prompts, lower-risk clinical summarization.

Option B — Dedicated reserved capacity- Requirements: BAA + reserved GPU pool; no shared tenant logging; audit logs accessible to MediWell; supports Token-level encryption with customer KMS.- Suitable for: workflows that may include limited identifiers but require predictable throughput and stronger isolation.

Option C — Private VPC / On-prem conduit- Requirements: BAA + network integration; optional on-prem package for air-gapped needs.- Suitable for: full-PHI workflows and long-term deployments where MediWell requires maximum control.

Attachments: I’ve uploaded a draft BAA for your review (Redwood_BAA_Draft.docx) and a short SOC2 summary (same as Ben attached).

Proposed immediate steps: 
- You: confirm whether PoC will use de-identified data (hosted OK) or identifiers (prefer Dedicated/Private).
- We: start filling your questionnaire and schedule a 30-min security call (Karthik + Redwood security lead).

If helpful, here’s the HubSpot deal link so legal/procurement can reference the scope: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567/MW-2026-031

Best,
Monica

--
Monica Patel
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwood.ai
Attachment: Redwood_BAA_Draft.docx (Word, 5 pages)


From: Jamie Torres <jamie.torres@mediwell.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Dr. Susan Park <susan.park@mediwell.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MediWell discovery follow-up — HIPAA, BAA, deployment modes (recap & asks)

Thanks Monica — looping in MediWell legal (legal@mediwell.com) to start the BAA/Master Services Agreement review. For timeline: procurement is aiming to have an initial contract in place by end of February if vendor paperwork + questionnaire are returned within two weeks.

A couple quick clarifications from procurement: 
- Preferred: Dedicated or Private VPC for any integration that may process identifiers.
- For the PoC we can do Hosted only if a strict de-identification workflow is documented and enforced.

Jamie

--
Jamie Torres
Senior Procurement Manager, MediWell Health
jamie.torres@mediwell.com


On Tue, 20 Jan 2026 at 09:12:00 -0800 Jamie Torres <jamie.torres@mediwell.com> wrote:
> Thanks Monica — looping in MediWell legal (legal@mediwell.com) to start the BAA/Master Services Agreement review. For timeline: procurement is aiming to have an initial contract in place by end of February if vendor paperwork + questionnaire are returned within two weeks.
