title: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)
Attachments: 2026-01-15-controlled-failover-metrics-snapshot.pdf, gameday-action-items-2026-01-16.csv

Team — sharing a concise leadership readout from Thursday’s controlled regional failover (us-east → eu-west) and what we’re doing next.

TL;DR
- The core automation path worked end-to-end (signal → controller decision → routing policy flip → traffic shift → stabilization).
- Customer impact was limited but not zero: brief elevated 5xx/timeouts for a small slice of Hosted traffic + streaming disconnects for some clients that don’t handle redirects cleanly.
- We found several “must-fix before wider rollout” items (anti-flap tuning, dashboard annotations, streaming reconnect guidance, and capacity headroom guardrails).

What we tested
- Scenario: planned impairment simulation in us-east and controlled failover to eu-west for a scoped set of routes/models and a limited tenant allowlist.
- Objective: validate (1) detection + decisioning speed, (2) safe automation/rollback behavior, (3) observability + on-call usability, and (4) Dedicated protection (no unexpected pool drain).

Results against success criteria (high level)
- Detection + failover time:
  - First corroborated impairment signal observed: ~T+75s from impairment start.
  - Policy flip executed: ~T+2m40s.
  - Traffic settled in eu-west: ~T+4–5m (client retry/backoff dominated tail).
- Error/latency impact:
  - Hosted API: short spike in 5xx/timeouts during the shift window; elevated tail latency in eu-west for ~12–18 minutes as retries + warm-up converged.
  - Dedicated: no unplanned customer pool drain; no sustained SLO-relevant impact observed.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - No uncontrolled oscillation, but we did see an automated rollback trigger earlier than intended due to hold-down/threshold interaction (details below).

Customer impact / support signals
- One customer reported transient 5xx/timeouts during the planned window (SUP-25310).
- Streaming disconnects were reported by a couple customers whose clients did not gracefully reconnect on redirect (SUP-25322).
- Follow-on ticket on elevated tail latency in eu-west immediately post-shift (SUP-25341) — correlates with headroom + retry storm.

What went well
- On-call coordination and go/no-go discipline were strong (checklist completion and rollback readiness were clear).
- Region health aggregation + policy engine evaluation produced explainable decisions (we could point to which signals tripped).
- Dedicated isolation worked as expected (Hosted shift did not inadvertently trigger Dedicated drain behaviors).

Issues found (key)
1) Observability gap: dashboards lacked consistent automated annotations for: impairment start, policy flip, rollback evaluation points. This made timeline reconstruction slower than it should be.
2) Anti-flap / rollback tuning: hold-down timer and corroboration windows were too aggressive in one segment, resulting in an early rollback evaluation while the region was still stabilizing.
3) Tail latency in eu-west after shift: headroom assumptions were optimistic for the specific routes/models in scope; combined with client retry behavior this caused a brief tail spike.
4) Streaming client behavior: redirect/reconnect handling still causes user-visible disconnects for some SDK versions / client patterns.

Decisions / next steps
- We are NOT expanding automation scope until the above items are closed and we pass a smaller-blast-radius rehearsal.
- We will schedule Game Day #2 with tighter allowlist + synthetic load shaping, and explicitly measure: failover time, retry amplification, and “time to steady-state p99”.

Action items (short list; full tracker linked)
- Add automation-driven dashboard annotations + incident timeline hooks (Owner: Sean / Observability; target: end of Jan)
- Retune rollback/hold-down thresholds and add “stabilization grace” logic (Owner: Rafael + Routing; target: end of Jan)
- Capacity guardrail: require minimum eu-west headroom for routes in allowlist before executing flip (Owner: Irene + Fleet; target: early Feb)
- Streaming: finalize redirect-safe reconnect behavior + publish SDK guidance (Owner: Priya + Runtime/Gateway; target: early Feb)
- Comms: align Support macros + status-page language for planned tests (Owner: Sean; target: before next test)

Links
- Results page (metrics + timeline): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Controlled+Failover+Test+Results+-+2026-01-15
- Draft postmortem (focus on regressions + prevention): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Postmortem%3A+Controlled+Failover+Test+Regression
- Action item tracker (ENG-2426): https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2426/post-gameday-action-items

Asks
1) Approval to hold Game Day #2 in early Feb (exact date TBD) once the above gating items are closed.
2) Confirmation that we should proactively notify the same set of enterprise accounts as last time (Support has the list), or expand/contract based on risk appetite.

Thanks to everyone who helped make the test controlled and safe — we learned the right things without a large blast radius.

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference


On Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 10:03 AM Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 10:03 AM PT
Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

This is the right framing.

Two adds:
- Let’s treat “dashboard annotations” as a hard gate for the next test. We lost time during the live window correlating graphs to events.
- Support comms: I’d prefer we keep proactive notifications limited to the same cohort (largest Hosted + Dedicated accounts + anyone with data residency questions) until streaming reconnect guidance is fully out.

I’ll sync with Rafael on gating criteria and get a proposed Feb window on the calendar once ENG-2426 is green.

Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 12:05 PM PT
Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

Answers inline.

1) SLO/error budget proximity
- We did not materially burn the multi-region availability SLO budget for the month.
- The spike was brief and scoped; the rolling burn-rate alerts triggered as expected, but did not cross paging thresholds for sustained windows.
- We’ll include the exact numbers in the postmortem once the annotation gap is fixed (so we can line up the windows precisely), but this was within the planned “acceptable test impact” bounds.

2) Preventing retry amplification in the target region (explicit go/no-go)
We’re adding three concrete gates before we run Game Day #2:
- Capacity/headroom gate: require minimum eu-west headroom for the exact route/model allowlist (not just aggregate regional headroom). Irene’s team is helping define thresholds.
- Gateway/backoff gate: validate retry budgets and Retry-After behavior in staging under redirect (Priya’s team + Peter’s circuit breaker changes), with a target “max retry amplification” metric.
- Synthetic load rehearsal: replay a shaped load pattern with perf canaries enabled and confirm p99 stabilization time is within target before touching prod.

If those three are green, we’ll propose an early Feb date with an even smaller allowlist than 1/15.

I’ll also make sure the go/no-go doc includes an explicit rollback trigger tied to retry amplification (not just error burn).

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference
text:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)
Attachments: 2026-01-15-controlled-failover-metrics-snapshot.pdf, gameday-action-items-2026-01-16.csv

Team — sharing a concise leadership readout from Thursday’s controlled regional failover (us-east → eu-west) and what we’re doing next.

TL;DR
- The core automation path worked end-to-end (signal → controller decision → routing policy flip → traffic shift → stabilization).
- Customer impact was limited but not zero: brief elevated 5xx/timeouts for a small slice of Hosted traffic + streaming disconnects for some clients that don’t handle redirects cleanly.
- We found several “must-fix before wider rollout” items (anti-flap tuning, dashboard annotations, streaming reconnect guidance, and capacity headroom guardrails).

What we tested
- Scenario: planned impairment simulation in us-east and controlled failover to eu-west for a scoped set of routes/models and a limited tenant allowlist.
- Objective: validate (1) detection + decisioning speed, (2) safe automation/rollback behavior, (3) observability + on-call usability, and (4) Dedicated protection (no unexpected pool drain).

Results against success criteria (high level)
- Detection + failover time:
  - First corroborated impairment signal observed: ~T+75s from impairment start.
  - Policy flip executed: ~T+2m40s.
  - Traffic settled in eu-west: ~T+4–5m (client retry/backoff dominated tail).
- Error/latency impact:
  - Hosted API: short spike in 5xx/timeouts during the shift window; elevated tail latency in eu-west for ~12–18 minutes as retries + warm-up converged.
  - Dedicated: no unplanned customer pool drain; no sustained SLO-relevant impact observed.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - No uncontrolled oscillation, but we did see an automated rollback trigger earlier than intended due to hold-down/threshold interaction (details below).

Customer impact / support signals
- One customer reported transient 5xx/timeouts during the planned window (SUP-25310).
- Streaming disconnects were reported by a couple customers whose clients did not gracefully reconnect on redirect (SUP-25322).
- Follow-on ticket on elevated tail latency in eu-west immediately post-shift (SUP-25341) — correlates with headroom + retry storm.

What went well
- On-call coordination and go/no-go discipline were strong (checklist completion and rollback readiness were clear).
- Region health aggregation + policy engine evaluation produced explainable decisions (we could point to which signals tripped).
- Dedicated isolation worked as expected (Hosted shift did not inadvertently trigger Dedicated drain behaviors).

Issues found (key)
1) Observability gap: dashboards lacked consistent automated annotations for: impairment start, policy flip, rollback evaluation points. This made timeline reconstruction slower than it should be.
2) Anti-flap / rollback tuning: hold-down timer and corroboration windows were too aggressive in one segment, resulting in an early rollback evaluation while the region was still stabilizing.
3) Tail latency in eu-west after shift: headroom assumptions were optimistic for the specific routes/models in scope; combined with client retry behavior this caused a brief tail spike.
4) Streaming client behavior: redirect/reconnect handling still causes user-visible disconnects for some SDK versions / client patterns.

Decisions / next steps
- We are NOT expanding automation scope until the above items are closed and we pass a smaller-blast-radius rehearsal.
- We will schedule Game Day #2 with tighter allowlist + synthetic load shaping, and explicitly measure: failover time, retry amplification, and “time to steady-state p99”.

Action items (short list; full tracker linked)
- Add automation-driven dashboard annotations + incident timeline hooks (Owner: Sean / Observability; target: end of Jan)
- Retune rollback/hold-down thresholds and add “stabilization grace” logic (Owner: Rafael + Routing; target: end of Jan)
- Capacity guardrail: require minimum eu-west headroom for routes in allowlist before executing flip (Owner: Irene + Fleet; target: early Feb)
- Streaming: finalize redirect-safe reconnect behavior + publish SDK guidance (Owner: Priya + Runtime/Gateway; target: early Feb)
- Comms: align Support macros + status-page language for planned tests (Owner: Sean; target: before next test)

Links
- Results page (metrics + timeline): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Controlled+Failover+Test+Results+-+2026-01-15
- Draft postmortem (focus on regressions + prevention): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Postmortem%3A+Controlled+Failover+Test+Regression
- Action item tracker (ENG-2426): https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2426/post-gameday-action-items

Asks
1) Approval to hold Game Day #2 in early Feb (exact date TBD) once the above gating items are closed.
2) Confirmation that we should proactively notify the same set of enterprise accounts as last time (Support has the list), or expand/contract based on risk appetite.

Thanks to everyone who helped make the test controlled and safe — we learned the right things without a large blast radius.

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference

title: Retargeting spend cap approval (Q1) — lifecycle growth sprint
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 16, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Retargeting spend cap approval (Q1) — lifecycle growth sprint

Camila — requesting approval for a Q1 retargeting test budget to support the Lifecycle Growth Experiments sprint (Hosted API activation/early retention).

Summary / ask
- Approve a Q1 spend cap of $25,000 total for retargeting (Jan–Mar), with a staged release:
  - Phase 1 (Sprint Week 3–4): $7,500 test budget
  - Phase 2 (Feb): up to $10,000 contingent on hitting measurement guardrails
  - Phase 3 (Mar): up to $7,500 contingent on continued performance + no sales conflicts

Why now / ROI rationale (brief)
- We have meaningful “signup → no activation” volume and a consistent drop-off between API key creation and first successful request.
- Retargeting is intended to recover stalled developers at low marginal engineering cost.
- We’ll optimize toward cost per activated developer (7-day activation) and early usage signals (first 100k tokens), not just CTR.
- Even modest lift (1–2pp) on activation in the “signup-no-activation” cohort should pay back quickly vs. the spend, given downstream conversion to usage-based revenue and sales-assisted handoffs.

Measurement expectations (what we will report)
- Primary: incremental lift in 7-day activation rate (first successful request) vs. holdout, attributed via UTMs + internal event joins.
- Secondary: time-to-first-token (TTFT), first 100k tokens within 7 days, D7 active usage.
- Efficiency: cost per activated developer (CPA-A), cost per 100k tokens milestone (CPA-100k), and spend pacing.

Guardrails / policies
- Hard exclusions to avoid targeting active enterprise opportunities:
  - Suppress any domain/account present in Sales “active opp” list (Jordan to confirm weekly refresh cadence)
  - Suppress known enterprise domains + high-intent inbound leads already in sales motion
  - Frequency caps to avoid overexposure
- Compliance: only first-party event-based audiences permitted per policy; no sensitive category targeting.

Attachments
- Q1_Retargeting_Budget_Request_RedwoodInference.pdf
- Retargeting_Audiences_v3.xlsx
- Measurement_Expectations_OnePager.pdf

If approved, Tyler (Marketing Ops) will set spend caps + UTMs and we’ll share a weekly readout in the sprint office hours.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Growth Marketing, Redwood Inference


---

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 16, 2026 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Retargeting spend cap approval (Q1) — lifecycle growth sprint

+1 with conditions on exclusions.

We can provide a weekly “active opp + sensitive accounts” suppression file (Mon AM). Two notes:
1) Please also exclude any accounts in late-stage security/procurement (even if they’re not marked “closed won” yet). We’ve had mixed reactions when ads show up during redlines.
2) Make sure we’re excluding by BOTH email domain and company name match where possible (some use personal domains early).

If we’re good on that, I’m aligned on the phased budget approach.

Jordan Blake
Sales Leadership, Redwood Inference


---

From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 16, 2026 at 11:21 AM
Subject: Re: Retargeting spend cap approval (Q1) — lifecycle growth sprint

Ben — thanks. I’m supportive in principle, but I want to be explicit on the “continue/stop” criteria before we open up Feb/Mar.

A few questions:
1) What’s the target threshold for CPA-A (cost per activated dev) that would justify Phase 2? And what would trigger an immediate pause?
2) How are we handling view-through vs. click-through attribution? I don’t want us over-claiming.
3) Reporting cadence: can we commit to a weekly snapshot + a final Q1 summary (spend, cohorts, incremental lift)?

Also: please confirm the daily spend cap during Phase 1 and confirm we can pause within 24 hours if Sales flags an issue.

Camila Reyes
Executive Sponsor (GTM), Redwood Inference


---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 16, 2026 at 1:48 PM
Subject: Re: Retargeting spend cap approval (Q1) — lifecycle growth sprint

All fair. Proposed decision criteria + pause rules below.

1) CPA-A thresholds
- Phase 1 target (learning): CPA-A <= $120 for the “signup-no-activation (7d)” audience.
- Continue to Phase 2 if BOTH:
  a) CPA-A <= $100 on a 7-day trailing basis AND
  b) we observe a measurable lift vs. holdout of >= +0.8pp activation (or a credible trend if sample size is still small)
- Immediate pause triggers:
  - CPA-A > $180 for 3 consecutive days, OR
  - complaint volume spikes (support tickets referencing ads / negative feedback), OR
  - any Sales escalations re: targeting (we will pause same-day)

2) Attribution approach
- We will not use platform-reported conversion claims as the source of truth.
- We’ll use UTMs on landing pages + internal event joins to measure: ad click → signup → activation.
- For view-through, we will report it separately as directional only (clearly labeled), and we won’t use it for go/no-go decisions.

3) Reporting
- Weekly: spend, audience breakdown, CPA-A, activation lift vs holdout, and any Sales/compliance notes.
- End of Q1: consolidated readout with clear “keep/iterate/kill” decision and next steps.

Spend / pacing
- Phase 1 daily cap: $500/day across platforms; total cap $7,500.
- Pause within 24 hours: yes. We can pause immediately; worst case is end-of-day billing.

Sales exclusion handling
- We’ll use Jordan’s weekly suppression file + an always-on exclusion list for enterprise domains.
- We’ll also exclude “in sales motion” users by CRM flag where available (Jordan/Isabelle can advise; we’ll keep it conservative).

If you’re good with the above, I’ll ask Ops to proceed with setup aligned to PM-670 and our UTM conventions.

Ben


---

From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 10:06 AM
Subject: Re: Retargeting spend cap approval (Q1) — lifecycle growth sprint

Approved with the structure you proposed:
- Q1 cap: $25,000 total
- Phase 1 release: up to $7,500, with $500/day cap
- Phase 2/3 contingent on the CPA-A thresholds + holdout lift reporting as described

Conditions:
1) Weekly report is mandatory (share in sprint office hours + email me the snapshot).
2) Maintain conservative exclusions (active opps + late-stage security/procurement) and honor any Sales pause request same-day.
3) Any material change to audiences or landing pages should be noted in the weekly report for measurement integrity.

Jordan — thanks for the suppression file offer; please set that cadence up with Ben starting Monday.

Camila

title: Vendor eval: lifecycle email tooling for Hosted API onboarding (questions + pricing)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alyssa Chen <alyssa.chen@sendcraft.io>
Cc: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>, Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Vendor eval: lifecycle email tooling for Hosted API onboarding (questions + pricing)

Hi Alyssa,

We’re evaluating lifecycle email tooling for a 4-week growth experiments sprint (self-serve onboarding + early retention) for our Hosted API.

We’re looking for:
- Triggered sequences based on product events (signup, created API key, first request, “first 100k tokens”)
- Segmentation + suppression logic (e.g., don’t email if already activated; suppression for enterprise opps/domains)
- Ability to run A/B tests with holdouts and export experiment results
- Solid deliverability tooling (warming/throttling, domain reputation monitoring, bounce/complaint dashboards)
- GDPR/CAN-SPAM compliance: unsubscribe + preferences, deletion workflows, data retention controls

Subject: Re: Vendor eval: lifecycle email tooling for Hosted API onboarding (questions + pricing)

Hi Michael (and Olga),

Happy to cover these:

1) Subprocessors: We maintain an up-to-date list here: https://sendcraft.io/security (Subprocessors section). We notify customers via email 30 days prior to adding a new subprocessor.
2) Data retention: Yes, retention can be configured separately for:
   - Event data (default 180d)
   - Contact records (until deleted; can be automatically removed after X days of inactivity)
   - Message logs (configurable; minimum 30d for deliverability and abuse prevention)
3) DSRs: Deletion requests via API or support ticket. We process within 7 days; immediate suppression is available at request time. After deletion, we retain non-identifying aggregate metrics and certain operational logs required for abuse prevention (no email address/user identifiers).
4) Tracking: Open tracking can be toggled per campaign/sequence. Click tracking is recommended but can be limited; we can discuss the tradeoffs.
5) Unsubscribe: Global unsubscribe is honored across all sends from a workspace/sender identity. We also support category-level preferences.
6) SOC 2: Yes, SOC 2 Type II. We can share the report via our trust portal under NDA. If you send your preferred NDA language, we can route it quickly.

Best,
Alyssa

title: Evaluation roadmap & throughput commitments for Q1 MAP
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@finco.com>
Date: Thu, 08 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Evaluation roadmap & throughput commitments for Q1 MAP

Hi Emily,

Thanks again for the time yesterday — following up with the proposed evaluation roadmap and the throughput targets we discussed. Attached is a working MAP (finco_map_v1.xlsx) and a short summary of the initial performance expectations so procurement has a sense of unit economics. Quick highlights: 

- Target sustained throughput: 500 QPS (inference requests) for the canonical payments use case
- Tokens/sec: target 20k tokens/sec steady-state for a 256-token average response
- Concurrency target: 50 concurrent request slots with tail-latency SLOs
- TTFT target: <= 75ms median; 95th < 250ms for the committed config
- Unit economics (hosted list-price estimate): ~$220 per 1M tokens; dedicated/reserved pricing TBD

Action items I propose for next week:
1) Redwood to run a 6-hour sustained throughput run with the FinCo prompt set (Ben)
2) Provide 95th and 99th latency histograms + tokens/sec breakouts (Ben/Marcus)
3) Procurement to confirm target contract date and any network/VPC requirements (Emily/procurement)
4) Schedule a 30-min review for results (suggest Tue 13 Jan)

Can you confirm the meeting time and whether procurement needs the cost breakdown in a different format?

Best,
Aditya Rao
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
redwood.ai

Attachments: finco_map_v1.xlsx (MAP spreadsheet)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 09 Jan 2026 14:20:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Evaluation roadmap & throughput commitments for Q1 MAP (test harness & costing)

Quick note on logistics — I can spin up the FinCo test harness on Dedicated Catalyst-8 (8xA100-style) tomorrow and run an initial 6-hour ramp to steady-state followed by a 2-hour steady run. Plan:

- Workload: FinCo prompt set (avg resp ~256 tokens; 60% short, 40% long)
- Ramp: 0 -> target QPS over 30 minutes to capture warm-up effects
- Metrics captured: tokens/sec, sustained QPS, median/95/99 latencies, TTFT, mem/KV cache hit rate
- Expected initial numbers (estimate based on similar workloads): tokens/sec ~18k-21k; sustained QPS 420-500; concurrency 35-50; 95th ~200-260ms; 99th could range 300-500ms depending on network tail.
- Rough costing estimate (on-demand): $0.22 per 1k tokens => ~$220 per 1M tokens (list). With a 30% reserved commitment, estimate drops to ~$154 / 1M tokens.

I'll attach the latency plots and the run plan after the first run (latency_plots_qps.pdf). If Procurement needs cost per million in a line item table I can export from the MAP spreadsheet.

Ben
SRE Lead, Redwood Inference

Attachments: latency_plots_qps.pdf (initial run plan)

Subject: Re: Evaluation roadmap & throughput commitments for Q1 MAP

Emily — attaching an updated MAP (finco_map_v1.xlsx) with concrete commitments from our side and a short results summary from Ben's first run: 

Summary of run (6h warm+2h steady):
- Sustained QPS observed: 450 (steady window averaged over 2h)
- Average tokens/sec: 18,000
- Median TTFT: 60ms; 95th latency: 210ms; 99th latency: 360ms
- Concurrency: 40 active slots during steady window
- Observed unit cost (on-demand): ~$220 / 1M tokens (matches estimate); projected reserved pricing ~ $150-160 / 1M tokens after commit

Committed actions in MAP:
1) Redwood to run a full 24-hour sustained throughput validation (Ben) — target date 13 Jan
2) Redwood to export 95/99 latency histograms + per-route token breakdowns for FinCo prompts (Marcus)
3) FinCo procurement to review the SOW stub and confirm PO timeline (Emily/procurement)
4) Final acceptance criteria: sustain >= 420 QPS for 24h with 99th latency < 400ms and unit cost <= $200 / 1M tokens (reserved pricing applied)

If procurement confirms the window, we'll start the 24-hour run on Tue 13 Jan and share the consolidated report by EOD Thu 15 Jan. Meeting remains Tue 13 Jan 11:00 PT.

Best,
Aditya

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@finco.com>, FinCo Procurement <procurement@finco.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 16:45:00 -0800
Subject: Final benchmark report and MAP update — sustained validation complete

Team,

We completed the 24-hour sustained validation window (24 Jan run started/ended as scheduled). Summary of final validated metrics and updated unit economics: 

Final validated performance (24h steady window):
- Sustained QPS (steady): 420
- Average tokens/sec: 16,800
- Median TTFT: 65ms; 95th latency: 245ms; 99th latency: 320ms
- Concurrency sustained: 38 active slots

Notes: tail latency improved vs initial noisy windows; the 99th settled around 320ms once KV cache warmed and request shaping was applied. We saw periodic spikes correlated with the FinCo long responses (top 10% of requests).

Updated unit economics (reserved capacity pricing applied):
- Effective cost (after 30% reserved discount + 10% volume rebate estimate): ~$190 per 1M tokens
- Projected monthly spend at target steady-state (420 QPS, avg 256 tokens, 30 days): ~ $77k/mo (reserve-adjusted) — details in attached MAP spreadsheet.

Attachments: finco_map_v1.xlsx, latency_plots_qps.pdf, redwood_nda_signed.pdf

Next steps / asks: 
1) Emily/procurement: please review the attached SOW stub and cost table and confirm PO send-by date (requested by 2026-01-22). DocuSign link: https://app.docusign.com/s/finco-sow-2026

- Workload: FinCo prompt set (avg resp ~256 tokens; 60% short, 40% long)

- Metrics captured: tokens/sec, sustained QPS, median/95/99 latencies, TTFT, mem/KV cache hit rate
- Expected initial numbers (estimate based on similar workloads): tokens/sec ~18k-21k; sustained QPS 420-500; concurrency 35-50; 95th ~200-260ms; 99th could range 300-500ms depending on network tail.
- Rough costing estimate (on-demand): $0.22 per 1k tokens => ~$220 per 1M tokens (list). With a 30% reserved commitment, estimate drops to ~$154 / 1M tokens.

Subject: Novalabs MAP & POC onramp — API keys, org setup, allowlist, peering

Hi Emily,

Thanks for the demo yesterday — appreciated the product questions from your infra and security folks. Per our conversation, I wanted to kick off a concise Mutual Action Plan (MAP) for the POC onramp so we have clear owners, dates, and the minimal items required for staging. Goal: have a sandbox-ready environment and API keys by the end of the week so your team can run the first eval load next week.

Proposed MAP items (high level):
1) Org & IAM seed
   - Redwood creates Novalabs org container (read-only view for your security) + initial service accounts.
   - Owner (Redwood): Ben Carter. Owner (Novalabs): Emily Zhou.
2) API keys & scopes
   - Short-lived API keys for evaluation, limited to text-generation and embeddings scopes.
   - Deliverable: keys, expiration, and sample cURL.
3) Network allowlist
   - IP ranges for our hosted endpoints; optional VPC peering for throughput-sensitive paths.
   - Owner (Novalabs): provide firewall contact and CIDR ranges.
4) VPC peering (optional but recommended for heavy token throughput)
   - Exchange contact details, ASN, and preferred peering window.

Can you confirm:
- Security contact (name + email) who will own allowlist/VPC peering?
- Whether short-lived keys are OK for the initial eval?
- Any blackout dates next week we should avoid for peering windows?

I attached a lightweight MAP draft (novalabs-map-v2.pdf) — it includes a recommended 5-day close timeline and sample ticket owners. If that looks good I’ll loop in Neha (security ops) and Priya (solutions engineering) to finalize the steps.

Thanks —
Ben

title: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19
Attachments: Optimize_Quality_Gates_Limited_Beta_Approval_Checklist_v3.pdf; limited-beta-route-list_2026-01-14.csv

Ava — requesting final leadership approval to start the limited beta for Optimize Quality Gates on hosted API routes the week of Jan 19.

What’s shipping in limited beta
- Enforce “quality gates required” for Optimize-suggested config diffs (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profile changes)
- Gate tiers:
  - Fast smoke gate first (small prompt subset) to catch obvious regressions quickly
  - Full gate required before any canary promotion to 100%
- Enforcement (defense-in-depth):
  - Optimize “Apply” endpoint blocks when gate = failed / incomplete
  - Rollout controller blocks canary promotion on gate failure and triggers rollback hooks
- Audit trail:
  - Gate run started/completed + pass/fail reasons
  - Override granted w/ expiration and reason

Proposed schedule
- Thu 1/16: enable gating enforcement in prod behind allowlist (routes/accounts only)
- Mon 1/19–Wed 1/21: onboard first 3 routes (internal + one friendly customer)
- Thu 1/22: expand to remaining allowlisted routes if pass rate + eval runtime are within bounds

Beta scope
- Hosted API only
- Allowlist routes/accounts in attached CSV (no auto-apply; one-click apply only)
- Explicit opt-in from CS for any external customer route

Sign-offs (collected offline; summarized in attached checklist)
- Applied ML (metrics/thresholds): approved pending flake monitoring + minimum sample sizes
- SRE (SLO/alerting): approved pending “gate execution latency” dashboard + alert thresholds
- Runtime (safety/knobs): approved w/ static validation pre-checks + guardrails on risky quant profile combos

Ask
- Can you reply with “Approved” (or conditions), so we can proceed with the 1/16 enablement and communicate to PM/SRE/Runtime?

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Product Lead, Optimize
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 10:05 AM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

A few checks before I sign off:

1) Overrides: who can approve during beta, and how are we preventing “rubber stamping” given the near-miss last month?
2) Is auto-apply fully disabled for allowlisted routes? I don’t want any background jobs applying changes without an explicit human in the loop.
3) Rollout coupling: is the rollout controller also enforcing, or are we relying only on Optimize API blocking?
4) What’s our go/no-go criteria for the 1/22 expansion (pass rate, flake rate, gate runtime)?

Send me answers + any conditions you want to attach, and I’ll respond with approval.

— Ava


---

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 11:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Answers inline.

1) Overrides
- Beta policy: overrides are allowed but time-bound (24h default; 72h max) and must include (a) reason code, (b) linked evidence/artifacts, (c) owner + follow-up ticket.
- Approvers (beta): oncall E7+ in Eng + one designated Product approver (me). No “self-approve” (requester != approver enforced).
- Every override emits an audit event and shows up in Console’s audit view; we’ll do a daily review during beta.
- If you want to tighten further, we can start with “no overrides for quantization profile changes” during week 1.

2) Auto-apply
- Confirmed: auto-apply is disabled for allowlisted routes for the duration of the limited beta. Optimize will still generate recommendations, but applying requires one-click + gate pass.

3) Enforcement location
- Both:
  - Optimize API blocks apply if gates failed/incomplete/expired
  - Rollout controller blocks canary promotion if gate != passed and will halt rollout + trigger rollback policy when configured
This is explicitly to avoid relying on a single enforcement point.

4) Go/no-go criteria for 1/22 expansion
Proposed thresholds (happy to adjust):
- Gate runtime P95: smoke <= 12 min; full <= 60 min (for the allowlisted routes)
- Flake rate: <= 3% (where flake = requires rerun due to variance / non-deterministic scoring)
- Gate pass rate: >= 85% for “safe” diffs (batching/cache). For quantization, expect lower initially; expansion depends on distribution.
- Zero incidents: no production canary promotion occurs with failed gates; no observed silent quality regression in online signals attributable to a gated change

If you’re OK with it, I’ll add “no overrides for quant changes in week 1” as a condition to keep risk low.

Aditya


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 12:18 PM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Approved with conditions:

- Limited beta only (allowlist as attached), hosted API only.
- Auto-apply remains disabled through the limited beta.
- Week 1: no overrides for quantization profile changes. If we need an exception, escalate to me with artifacts.
- Daily report (Mon–Fri) to me: gate run counts, pass/fail breakdown, flake rate, P95 runtime (smoke + full), and any overrides/audit entries.
- 1/22 expansion requires meeting your thresholds AND an explicit go/no-go check with SRE on gate latency + regional capacity.

Please make sure the rollout comms call out what happens on gate failures (blocked apply + rollout halt) so CS isn’t surprised.

— Ava


---

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 8:41 AM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Confirmed on all conditions.

I’ll:
- Keep auto-apply disabled for the allowlist through the limited beta
- Enforce “no overrides for quant profile changes” in week 1 (override request path will hard-block with a message to escalate)
- Send the daily report starting Monday 1/19
- Schedule a 15-min go/no-go on Thu 1/22 with SRE for runtime/region capacity + thresholds review

Re: CS comms — agreed. I’ll update the internal beta notes today with:
- What users see when a gate fails (blocked apply, reasons, artifact links)
- What happens during rollout (canary halt, rollback hooks)
- How to request an override (and that quant overrides are disabled week 1)

Thanks,
Aditya

title: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions
preview:
From: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for the Private deployment deep dive yesterday.

As part of our security review, can you share:

1) Your audit log event schema (field list + required vs optional). We need to confirm actor attribution, tenant/project scoping, and correlation IDs.
2) Which events are guaranteed to be logged for "sensitive actions" (RBAC changes, API key lifecycle, export destination changes, policy/routing changes, retention/residency settings, and Private install/upgrade actions).
3) Export options (Splunk HEC and Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics preferred). Any notes on batching, retries, and failure behavior.
4) Delivery semantics: best-effort vs at-least-once; how duplicates are represented; whether there is an idempotency key.
5) Data handling: any PII in audit logs, redaction rules, and retention controls in Private.

If you have a sample event payload for a couple of actions (e.g., role change + API key rotation), that would help our SIEM team validate mappings.

Best,
Dana Whitaker
Director, Security Engineering
Helio Bank


---

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
Cc: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions
Attachments: redwood-audit-log-schema-v1.json; redwood-siem-export-guide-draft-v0.9.pdf

Dana,

Sharing two attachments:

- redwood-audit-log-schema-v1.json (canonical schema v1; includes required/optional fields and constraints)
- redwood-siem-export-guide-draft-v0.9.pdf (SIEM export guidance; Splunk HEC + Azure Sentinel notes, plus recommended de-dupe)

Answers inline below to match your questions.

1) Schema (required vs optional)

At a high level, every audit event includes:
- schema_version (e.g., "1.0")
- event_id (unique ID for de-duplication; globally unique within a deployment)
- event_type (enumerated string; consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private for equivalent actions)
- occurred_at (RFC3339 timestamp)
- outcome ("success" | "failure")
- actor.* (who performed the action)
- target.* (who/what was acted on)
- resource.* (the affected object/scope)
- tenant_id + optional project_id (scoping)
- source_ip (when available; see note below)
- correlation IDs: request_id and trace_id (when action is triggered via API/Console)

For actor attribution we support:
- actor.type: user | service_account | api_key | system
- actor.id: stable identifier
- actor.display_name: optional (we recommend using this for convenience only; do not depend on it)
- actor.impersonated_by.*: optional; present when an action was taken via delegated/impersonation flows (where applicable)

2) Sensitive actions coverage

Our current standard is to emit audit events for the following categories (not exhaustive list, but the major buckets you called out):
- RBAC: role create/update/delete, role assignment changes, permission policy updates
- Credentials: API key create/rotate/revoke; token issuance/config changes
- Policy/routing: routing policy updates, fallback policy changes, allow/deny list updates (where used)
- Data controls: retention setting changes, region/residency setting changes
- Audit pipeline: audit export destination create/update/delete; exporter enable/disable
- Private operations: install, upgrade, rollback, config apply, and credential rotation events from the Private installer

We’ll also include outcome="failure" for denied/failed attempts (e.g., unauthorized RBAC mutation), with error_class/error_code fields (no secrets).

3) SIEM export (Splunk + Sentinel)

- Splunk: HTTP Event Collector (HEC) target is supported. We batch events and retry on transient failures.
- Azure Sentinel: Log Analytics ingestion is supported (for Sentinel consumption). We batch and retry similarly.

Exporter behavior notes:
- Retries use exponential backoff; events are not dropped silently.
- If the exporter destination is down, the exporter will buffer up to a configured limit; once exceeded, it enters a "degraded" state and surfaces metrics/alerts (details in the guide).

4) Delivery semantics / duplicates / idempotency

We design delivery as at-least-once from producer to exporter, which can result in duplicates during retries.

How we recommend handling this:
- De-duplicate on event_id.
- event_id is a stable identifier for the logical event (i.e., retries preserve the same event_id).

We do not recommend attempting to de-dupe on timestamps or message contents.

5) Data handling (PII/redaction/retention)

We minimize PII in audit logs by design:
- We do not log secrets (API keys, tokens, raw credentials, request bodies containing prompts, etc.).
- Fields that could contain sensitive identifiers are either omitted or represented as stable IDs.
- Free-form strings are constrained; event_type and actor/target types are enumerated.

Retention in Private:
- Retention is configurable by the customer for the in-cluster audit store (where used).
- Exported events are governed by your SIEM retention policies.

Source IP note:
- source_ip is populated when present in the ingress context (API Gateway / Console). For certain installer-initiated actions in fully offline environments, source_ip may be unavailable; in those cases we still include operator identity where available and a local correlation id.

Sample events

We can share sample payloads for:
- rbac.role_assignment.updated
- credential.api_key.rotated

If you confirm your preferred format (JSON lines vs single JSON object per event) we’ll send a couple representative samples that match your target.

If it helps, I can also set up a 30-min working session with your SIEM engineer + our Security Engineering owner (Marcus, cc’d) to walk through field mapping.

Regards,
Aisha Rahman
Office of the CISO
Redwood Inference


---

From: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 11:21 AM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Thanks, this is helpful.

Two follow-ups:

1) You mentioned event_id is stable across retries. Is that true across service boundaries as well (e.g., Console -> control plane -> exporter), or only within the exporter?

2) For role changes, do you log the before/after diff? If yes, is it structured or a string? We want to avoid high-cardinality fields in Splunk.

Also, JSON lines (one event per line) is easiest for us.

-Raj


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 12:07 PM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Raj,

On (1): event_id is generated at event creation time by the producing service and preserved end-to-end through our pipeline (Console/control plane/installer -> audit ingestion -> exporter). Retries in the exporter do not mint a new ID.

We also include correlation fields to help tie together multi-hop actions:
- request_id (ingress request)
- trace_id (distributed trace, if enabled)
- admin_action_id (used for certain Console-admin workflows that fan out into multiple underlying mutations)

On (2): we include a constrained “change summary” for sensitive config changes.

For RBAC/role changes specifically:
- We include a structured summary when feasible (e.g., lists of permission identifiers added/removed), using enumerated permission IDs rather than free-form text.
- We avoid embedding full object snapshots in audit logs.
- We cap list sizes and truncate with an explicit flag when the change set is large (to protect cardinality and payload size).

If you’d like, we can provide a sample rbac.role_assignment.updated event with the change_summary fields populated so you can validate indexing in Splunk.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Security Engineering (Exec Owner)
Redwood Inference

text:
From: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for the Private deployment deep dive yesterday.

As part of our security review, can you share:

1) Your audit log event schema (field list + required vs optional). We need to confirm actor attribution, tenant/project scoping, and correlation IDs.
2) Which events are guaranteed to be logged for "sensitive actions" (RBAC changes, API key lifecycle, export destination changes, policy/routing changes, retention/residency settings, and Private install/upgrade actions).
3) Export options (Splunk HEC and Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics preferred). Any notes on batching, retries, and failure behavior.
4) Delivery semantics: best-effort vs at-least-once; how duplicates are represented; whether there is an idempotency key.
5) Data handling: any PII in audit logs, redaction rules, and retention controls in Private.

If you have a sample event payload for a couple of actions (e.g., role change + API key rotation), that would help our SIEM team validate mappings.

Best,
Dana Whitaker
Director, Security Engineering
Helio Bank


---

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
Cc: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions
Attachments: redwood-audit-log-schema-v1.json; redwood-siem-export-guide-draft-v0.9.pdf

Dana,

Sharing two attachments:

- redwood-audit-log-schema-v1.json (canonical schema v1; includes required/optional fields and constraints)
- redwood-siem-export-guide-draft-v0.9.pdf (SIEM export guidance; Splunk HEC + Azure Sentinel notes, plus recommended de-dupe)

Answers inline below to match your questions.

1) Schema (required vs optional)

At a high level, every audit event includes:
- schema_version (e.g., "1.0")
- event_id (unique ID for de-duplication; globally unique within a deployment)
- event_type (enumerated string; consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private for equivalent actions)
- occurred_at (RFC3339 timestamp)
- outcome ("success" | "failure")
- actor.* (who performed the action)
- target.* (who/what was acted on)
- resource.* (the affected object/scope)
- tenant_id + optional project_id (scoping)
- source_ip (when available; see note below)
- correlation IDs: request_id and trace_id (when action is triggered via API/Console)

For actor attribution we support:
- actor.type: user | service_account | api_key | system
- actor.id: stable identifier
- actor.display_name: optional (we recommend using this for convenience only; do not depend on it)
- actor.impersonated_by.*: optional; present when an action was taken via delegated/impersonation flows (where applicable)

2) Sensitive actions coverage

Our current standard is to emit audit events for the following categories (not exhaustive list, but the major buckets you called out):
- RBAC: role create/update/delete, role assignment changes, permission policy updates
- Credentials: API key create/rotate/revoke; token issuance/config changes
- Policy/routing: routing policy updates, fallback policy changes, allow/deny list updates (where used)
- Data controls: retention setting changes, region/residency setting changes
- Audit pipeline: audit export destination create/update/delete; exporter enable/disable
- Private operations: install, upgrade, rollback, config apply, and credential rotation events from the Private installer

We’ll also include outcome="failure" for denied/failed attempts (e.g., unauthorized RBAC mutation), with error_class/error_code fields (no secrets).

3) SIEM export (Splunk + Sentinel)

- Splunk: HTTP Event Collector (HEC) target is supported. We batch events and retry on transient failures.
- Azure Sentinel: Log Analytics ingestion is supported (for Sentinel consumption). We batch and retry similarly.

Exporter behavior notes:
- Retries use exponential backoff; events are not dropped silently.
- If the exporter destination is down, the exporter will buffer up to a configured limit; once exceeded, it enters a "degraded" state and surfaces metrics/alerts (details in the guide).

4) Delivery semantics / duplicates / idempotency

We design delivery as at-least-once from producer to exporter, which can result in duplicates during retries.

How we recommend handling this:
- De-duplicate on event_id.
- event_id is a stable identifier for the logical event (i.e., retries preserve the same event_id).

We do not recommend attempting to de-dupe on timestamps or message contents.

5) Data handling (PII/redaction/retention)

We minimize PII in audit logs by design:
- We do not log secrets (API keys, tokens, raw credentials, request bodies containing prompts, etc.).
- Fields that could contain sensitive identifiers are either omitted or represented as stable IDs.
- Free-form strings are constrained; event_type and actor/target types are enumerated.

Retention in Private:
- Retention is configurable by the customer for the in-cluster audit store (where used).
- Exported events are governed by your SIEM retention policies.

Source IP note:
- source_ip is populated when present in the ingress context (API Gateway / Console). For certain installer-initiated actions in fully offline environments, source_ip may be unavailable; in those cases we still include operator identity where available and a local correlation id.

Sample events

We can share sample payloads for:
- rbac.role_assignment.updated
- credential.api_key.rotated

If you confirm your preferred format (JSON lines vs single JSON object per event) we’ll send a couple representative samples that match your target.

If it helps, I can also set up a 30-min working session with your SIEM engineer + our Security Engineering owner (Marcus, cc’d) to walk through field mapping.

Regards,
Aisha Rahman
Office of the CISO
Redwood Inference


---

From: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 11:21 AM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Thanks, this is helpful.

Two follow-ups:

1) You mentioned event_id is stable across retries. Is that true across service boundaries as well (e.g., Console -> control plane -> exporter), or only within the exporter?

2) For role changes, do you log the before/after diff? If yes, is it structured or a string? We want to avoid high-cardinality fields in Splunk.

Also, JSON lines (one event per line) is easiest for us.

-Raj


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 12:07 PM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Raj,

On (1): event_id is generated at event creation time by the producing service and preserved end-to-end through our pipeline (Console/control plane/installer -> audit ingestion -> exporter). Retries in the exporter do not mint a new ID.

We also include correlation fields to help tie together multi-hop actions:
- request_id (ingress request)
- trace_id (distributed trace, if enabled)
- admin_action_id (used for certain Console-admin workflows that fan out into multiple underlying mutations)

On (2): we include a constrained “change summary” for sensitive config changes.

For RBAC/role changes specifically:
- We include a structured summary when feasible (e.g., lists of permission identifiers added/removed), using enumerated permission IDs rather than free-form text.
- We avoid embedding full object snapshots in audit logs.
- We cap list sizes and truncate with an explicit flag when the change set is large (to protect cardinality and payload size).

If you’d like, we can provide a sample rbac.role_assignment.updated event with the change_summary fields populated so you can validate indexing in Splunk.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Security Engineering (Exec Owner)
Redwood Inference


---

To: Dana Whitaker <dana.whitaker@heliobank.com>
Cc: Raj Mehta <raj.mehta@heliobank.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 2:26 PM
Subject: Re: Helio Bank security review: audit log schema + SIEM export questions

Dana/Raj,

Confirmed on JSON lines — that’s also our recommended format for SIEM pipelines.

Next steps:
- We’ll send two JSONL sample events (RBAC assignment update + API key rotation) aligned to schema v1 with change_summary populated.

title: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 8:17 AM PST
Subject: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Hi Neha,

Sharing a short exec readout on the “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” work as we start the first full sweep week. The team is on track on harness/workloads, but we’re bottlenecked on variance + benchmark fleet capacity stability.

Attachment: Q1-2026-Perf-Baseline-Exec-Readout-v1.pdf
Attachment: benchmark-cluster-capacity-plan-q1-2026.xlsx

Summary status (as of Jan 12)
- Representative workload suite v1: implemented for chat streaming, long-context prefill, embeddings. Tool-calling workload is next and not blocking the first baseline freeze.
- Perf harness: run manifest validator is merged + wired into CI for the benchmark repo; we can now attribute changes to driver/runtime/scheduler/kernel selection reliably.
- Baseline sweep: first partial sweep completed (us-east only) for top two model families on H100. eu-west runs are blocked by fleet availability and some IAM friction.

Key risks
1) Variance/noise is still too high to set regression thresholds confidently
   - We’re seeing ~6–12% p99 TTFT swing run-to-run on long-prefill on shared nodes (most correlated with GPU clock variability + co-tenancy).
   - Warmup is now standardized, but we still need consistent isolation (or a “benchmark mode” scheduling window).
   - Without addressing this, regression gating will either page constantly or be too lax.

2) Benchmark fleet capacity and access
   - Current reservation model is “best effort,” leading to missed windows and mixed hardware (especially for eu-west).
   - IAM/service account permissions for artifact export + node-level controls are not fully unblocked (tracking in INT-8821).

3) Timeline risk to baseline freeze
   - If we can’t stabilize variance and secure dedicated windows, we risk slipping the baseline anchor date by ~1–2 weeks, which cascades into release gating adoption.

Asks / decisions needed this week
A) Approve dedicated benchmark windows (2 weeks) for baseline freeze
   - H100: us-east + eu-west (priority)
   - A100/L40S: us-east (secondary; can be staggered)
   - Goal: consistent hardware + isolation to lock the “golden” Q1 baseline numbers.

B) Approve incremental GPU-hour budget buffer
   - We need headroom for reruns while calibrating thresholds (noise model needs more samples per cohort).
   - See attached capacity plan sheet; the ask is to approve the “calibration buffer” line item.

C) Support priority to unblock access + stability
   - Fast-track INT-8821 (service account + node controls).
   - Align on whether we can temporarily pause lower-priority internal GPU consumers during reserved windows.

If helpful, I can do a 15-minute walkthrough. If you’re aligned, I’ll ask the team to treat Jan 24 as the tentative “baseline anchor build” date, contingent on the reservation + IAM items.

Thanks,
Ava

—
Ava Chen
Director, Infrastructure Programs
Redwood Inference


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 9:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Thanks, Ava.

On the asks:
- Dedicated windows: Approved in principle. Please send me the exact SKU x region window proposal (dates/times) and I’ll have SRE/infra enforce the reservation blocks.
- Budget buffer: Approved for the calibration buffer in the attached sheet. Finance doesn’t need extra ceremony for this; just keep it to the plan.
- INT-8821: I’ll ping the IAM/infra owner today to get this unblocked. If it’s not resolved by EOD tomorrow, escalate back to me and we’ll treat it as a stop-the-line issue for the baseline work.

Re: pausing other internal GPU consumers — I’m OK doing this during reserved windows if we can communicate 48 hours ahead and limit to 2 weeks.

Let’s do 15 minutes tomorrow. Include the proposed baseline anchor date and what “done” means for the freeze.

Neha

—
Neha Kapoor
VP, Infrastructure & Reliability
Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 10:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Great — thank you.

Proposed reservation blocks (baseline freeze sprint)
- us-east / H100: Jan 13–Jan 24, 6pm–6am PT (nightly), plus Sat Jan 17 full-day for the long-prefill cohorts
- eu-west / H100: Jan 14–Jan 24, local overnight windows (we can match 7pm–7am CET)
- us-east / A100: Jan 19–Jan 23, 6pm–6am PT (only after H100 cohorts are stable)

Definition of baseline freeze “done” (for Jan 24 anchor)
- For each baseline cohort (model family x workload x region x SKU):
  - p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT with stable variance bands
  - throughput (tokens/sec and req/sec where applicable)
  - run manifest + env capture stored and queryable
  - cold vs warm cache scenarios recorded (decision already made to report both)
- Noise model calibrated enough to set initial gating thresholds (with “warn” vs “fail” separation)

For the 15-minute tomorrow: I’ll bring a 1-slide view of current variance drivers and what we expect to change with isolation/clock controls.

Also, re: INT-8821 — the immediate pain is artifact export + node-level settings for clock locking. If we can’t get clock locking, we can still run, but the variance bands widen and gating becomes less useful.

Thanks again,
Ava


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 2:06 PM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Approved on those blocks.

Action items:
1) Send the reservation blocks to SRE scheduling + benchmark fleet owner today (cc me on the note).
2) Put the “48-hour heads up” comms in place for the internal GPU pause windows (keep it tight and time-bound).
3) For tomorrow’s 15 min: please include a recommendation on the minimum acceptable variance to start gating (even if we start in “warn-only” mode).

Re: clock locking — I agree it’s worth doing for the baseline suite; let’s treat it as required for H100 baseline runs.

Neha

title: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions
preview:
From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions
Attachments: optimization-audit-events-v1.0.docx, audit-log-payload-examples-optimization.txt

Marissa — completed security review of the Optimization Config audit event taxonomy (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback). Overall direction looks good, but we need a few changes before I can sign off for Limited GA.

Top feedback (required):

1) Event naming + lifecycle coverage
- Please standardize to a single namespace and tense. Proposed:
  - optimization_config.version_created
  - optimization_config.version_validated
  - optimization_config.rollout_started
  - optimization_config.rollout_paused
  - optimization_config.rollout_gate_failed
  - optimization_config.rollout_promoted
  - optimization_config.rollback_requested
  - optimization_config.rollback_completed
- We need BOTH “requested” and “completed” for rollback. “Completed” must reflect actual control-plane state change to prior active version (not just that an operator clicked a button).

2) Required fields (minimum) for enterprise auditability
For every event above, please ensure these are ALWAYS present (even on failures):
- event_id (uuid), event_time (RFC3339), event_name, event_schema_version
- actor_type (human | service), actor_id (user id / service id), actor_email (if human), actor_ip, user_agent (if available)
- authz_decision (allow/deny) and authz_policy_version (so we can prove RBAC enforcement)
- request_id and trace_id (or equivalent correlation IDs)
- environment (hosted | dedicated | private)
- tenant_id
- scope_model (model id/name), scope_region, scope_deployment_id (if applicable)
- config_object_id (Optimization Config object), config_version_id (immutable version), and previous_active_version_id (where applicable)
- result (success|failure) + error_code/error_summary (for failure)

3) “Blast radius” + intent
For apply/promote/rollback events, we need fields that capture what was intended:
- rollout_id
- rollout_strategy (all-at-once | canary_percent | shard)
- canary_percent (if applicable)
- change_reason (free text) and change_ticket (Linear/Jira key) — strongly preferred; if empty, include “unspecified” (don’t omit)

4) Data handling
- Do NOT log config payloads or diffs in audit logs. Metadata only. Payload/diff should remain in the config service with access controls.
- Ensure no prompt content, request bodies, or customer data appears in audit log fields.

5) Retention + export
- Default retention: 400 days in Redwood audit store.
- SIEM export: these events must be part of the export stream (same schema) and stable (schema_version increments only).

Sign-off conditions for Limited GA (Hosted/Dedicated):
- Events emitted for: create, validate, rollout start, gate failure, promote, rollback request, rollback completion.
- Each event includes the “Required fields” above (we’ll spot-check payloads).
- At least one automated test that asserts rollback emits both events and includes previous_active_version_id.
- Documentation: a short section in the customer-facing audit log docs describing these events and field meanings.

If you want, we can do a 20-min working session early next week to converge on the final taxonomy. For now, please reply with (a) what you’ll change vs defer and (b) targeted landing date for ENG-4826 / PR-19941.

— Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
CISO, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Thanks — this is clear.

A couple clarifications so we implement what you expect:
1) For “actor_ip” and “user_agent”: do you want those for both Console-triggered actions and API-triggered actions? (Console backend can pass through request metadata; internal automation may not.)
2) For Private: we can support the same taxonomy, but the “actor_*” fields may be the operator’s identity only if they’re authenticating via the private control plane (depends on customer setup). Is it acceptable to mark actor_type=service with actor_id=installer/helm for some cases?
3) “change_ticket”: should we require format validation (e.g., LINEAR-1234 / ENG-####), or just best-effort string?

We’re trying to keep the surface area small for Limited GA but I agree rollback_requested + rollback_completed is important.

Marissa


From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 10:21 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

+1 to Marissa’s questions.

From release side: can you confirm whether audit event completeness is a hard go/no-go for enabling the rollback feature flag in prod?

If yes, I’d like to add a release train check item:
- “audit event schema_version=v1.0 present in prod logs for apply + rollback”

Also: do you care about a backfill/migration event when we convert existing (unversioned) optimization configs into versioned records?

Connor
Release Engineering Lead


From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 11:07 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Replies inline.

On Jan 10, 2026 at 10:03 AM, Marissa Cole wrote:
> 1) For “actor_ip” and “user_agent”: do you want those for both Console-triggered actions and API-triggered actions?

Yes where available. Requirement is: fields must exist; values may be “unknown” / “not_collected” for system-initiated actions. Please don’t omit the keys entirely (customers treat omission as “bug / missing audit detail”).

> 2) For Private… acceptable to mark actor_type=service with actor_id=installer/helm?

Yes. For Private deployments we should be explicit:
- actor_type=service
- actor_id=redwood-private-installer / helm-controller (or similar)
- include “operator_identity” as a single string if you have it (e.g., SSO subject), but do NOT block Private on this for Limited GA.

> 3) “change_ticket” format validation?

Best-effort string for v1.0. If validation is easy, we can warn but not reject.

On Jan 10, 2026 at 10:21 AM, Connor O'Brien wrote:
> can you confirm whether audit event completeness is a hard go/no-go for enabling the rollback feature flag in prod?

For Hosted/Dedicated Limited GA: yes, it’s a go/no-go. Rollback without a reliable audit trail is a non-starter for a chunk of enterprise prospects (and it’s a core control for change management).

> do you care about a backfill/migration event when we convert existing configs?

Yes, but it can be a separate event name and not block Limited GA if it’s clearly documented as “migration_only”. Suggested:
- optimization_config.migration_completed (include counts + time window)

Aisha


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 2:36 PM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Aisha — action plan proposal based on your notes (so we can align on the GA gate):

We will do for Limited GA:
- Adopt event namespace exactly as you suggested (optimization_config.*) and lock schema_version=v1.0.
- Emit: version_created, version_validated, rollout_started, rollout_gate_failed, rollout_promoted, rollback_requested, rollback_completed.
- Ensure required fields always present; when unknown we’ll populate “not_collected”.
- Add previous_active_version_id to promote + rollback_completed.
- Add a basic contract test in CI asserting both rollback events are emitted and include request_id/trace_id.
- Add doc section to the audit log page (link in your email).

We will defer (post-Limited GA):
- Detailed “gate_results” payload beyond pass/fail summary (we’ll keep pass/fail + gate_set_id for now).
- Private installer enrichment for operator identity beyond actor_type=service.

ETA:
- Target merge of audit log changes: end of next week (Fri 1/17) pending PR review.
- Release train item (Connor): add to the go/no-go checklist with a prod validation step.

Please confirm this meets your sign-off conditions.

Ava
Chief of Staff to Engineering (interim)


From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 4:41 PM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Confirmed — the plan as written meets security sign-off requirements for Limited GA, with two notes:

1) For rollout_started / rollout_promoted / rollback_* events, please include rollout_strategy + canary_percent (even if null). This is one of the first questions auditors ask when investigating “who changed prod and how broadly”.

2) Please ensure authz_decision + authz_policy_version are present not just on “deny” outcomes, but on allowed actions too.

Connor — yes, please add the prod validation step to the release checklist. If it helps, security can provide a sample Splunk query once the event_name and schema_version are final.

Once PR is merged, tag me on the doc update for a quick review. After that, consider security unblocked for enabling the feature flag from an audit-logging perspective.

— Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
CISO, Redwood Inference
