Subject: RE: ACME Order Form redlines — liability carve-outs & breach sub-cap

Laura / Rishi / Karthik — FYI inbound from ACME (attached). Procurement wants a quick yes/no on the $5M breach-specific sub-cap and confirmation that the Security Addendum can define scope. Can you please: 

1) Laura and Rishi — draft 2 short options (customer-friendly and sales-friendly) for MSA language that we can share back to Julia. Call out: whether the sub-cap should be \"lesser of\" the general cap or a standalone cap, and whether we can preserve consequential damages exclusion while permitting third-party remediation costs.
2) Karthik — what commercial concession would you need if we accept a $5M sub-cap (e.g., price premium, one-time credit, adjusted SLA credits)? Also advise whether this impacts the committed capacity / discount bands.

ben.carter@redwood.com


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Sam Huang <sam.huang@acme.com>, Julia Park <julia.park@wellslegal.com>
Date: 2025-11-06T14:32:00-08:00
Subject: RE: ACME Order Form redlines — liability carve-outs & breach sub-cap

Ben / Team — reviewed ACME's redline and Security Addendum draft. Below are two workable tracks and the pros/cons for each. I recommend we pick one and return a clean redline to Julia.

OPTION 1 (Conservative/legal preferred):
- Language: Add an explicit, incident-specific sub-cap: \"Notwithstanding anything to the contrary, for any Security Incident (as defined in the Security Addendum), Customer's aggregate recovery for claims arising from such Security Incident shall not exceed the lesser of (a) $5,000,000 and (b) the aggregate liability cap set forth in the Agreement.\"
- Consequential Damages: Keep broad exclusion for \"consequential, special, punitive\" damages, but carve out direct third-party costs reasonably incurred for customer notification, credit monitoring, and regulatory fines to the extent caused by our breach and not otherwise recoverable from insurers or third parties.
- Security Addendum: Accept incorporation by reference, but require the Addendum to contain specific definitions of \"Security Incident\", \"Notification\" and \"Mitigation\" and a requirement for prompt cooperation. Add a flow-down that remedies under the Addendum (e.g., incident credits, forensic costs) are primary and reduce any recoverable damages under the sub-cap.

Pros: limits Redwood exposure; ties recovery mechanics to the Addendum; defensible in litigation.
Cons: customer may push back on \"lesser of\" formulation and on carveback language.

OPTION 2 (Commercial/sales preferred):
- Language: Standalone sub-cap: \"For Security Incidents as defined in the Security Addendum, Customer's aggregate recovery shall not exceed $5,000,000.\" No \"lesser of\" tie to the general cap.
- Consequential Damages: Keep exclusion but list permitted categories for remediation and regulatory response (notification, forensics, credit monitoring).
- Security Addendum: Accept incorporation by reference but propose a short set of guaranteed controls and a 30-day cure timeline to trigger the sub-cap.

Pros: more customer-friendly and easier to close; cleaner number on the table.
Cons: increases Redis exposure if our general cap is lower than $5M and could be seen as a standalone ceiling that may exceed intended risk appetite.

Draft redline language attached in a short text file (redline_summary_20251112.txt) and noted where to insert into MSA / Order Form. 

Question for Sales: Is $5M a hard floor for ACME or a negotiation opener? Karthik, need customer tolerance and whether there is flexibility on non-monetary remedies (extended SLA credits, pilot guarantees, or indemnity modifications).

If you want, I can call Julia to walk through the legal rationale tomorrow.

—Laura
Laura Bennett
Head of Contracts & Commercial Counsel
laura.bennett@redwood.com


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Cc: Sam Huang <sam.huang@acme.com>, Julia Park <julia.park@wellslegal.com>
Date: 2025-11-07T09:18:00-08:00
Subject: RE: ACME Order Form redlines — liability carve-outs & breach sub-cap

Quick commercial read: procurement is firm on $5M as the target number; I don't think they'll move below $4M. Options to trade: (a) accept the standalone $5M sub-cap but add a 15% uplift to list prices for the committed term (or equivalent one-time implementation fee), or (b) keep existing pricing and limit carvebacks for regulatory fines (i.e., allow notification/forensics but exclude fines unless willful misconduct).

From closing timeline perspective: ACME procurement wants a signed Order Form by 11/21 to hit their internal quarter close. If legal gives us two clean paths (Option 1 and Option 2) I'll present both to ACME.

One more ask — if we do Option 2 (standalone $5M) we should also require a mutual cap on customer indemnities for Redwood data misuse claims to keep balance. Laura — can you draft a mirror clause?

Thanks
Karthik Iyer
Senior Account Executive
karthik.iyer@redwood.com


From: Julia Park <julia.park@wellslegal.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-11-12T16:47:00-08:00
Subject: RE: ACME Order Form redlines — liability carve-outs & breach sub-cap

Team — thanks for the fast turnaround and the two option tracks. ACME procurement confirms they can accept \"Option 1\" only if Redwood agrees to the $5M number as a standalone figure (i.e., they reject the \"lesser of\" language) but are willing to accept stronger flow-downs to the Security Addendum (they need it to trigger the sub-cap).

ACME's counter-proposal (short):
1) Sub-cap: \"For Security Incidents, Customer's aggregate recovery shall not exceed $5,000,000.\"
2) Consequential/exclusion: Maintain the exclusion but permit recovery of direct third-party remediation costs (reasonable notification & credit monitoring) and fines/penalties to the extent they are final and not otherwise covered by insurance; Redwood may require mitigation credits to be deducted from recoverable amounts.
3) Security Addendum: ACME will finalize the Addendum with explicit definitions and a requirement for Redwood to follow the incident response playbook; they ask Redwood to accept this as the controlling definition for the sub-cap.

Attached is ACME's updated redline (ACME_OrderForm_Redline_v2.docx) with the limited changes. If Redwood accepts the standalone $5M sub-cap and the Addendum tie-in, ACME will proceed to final procurement approval and send the DocuSign link (we can execute Order Form first, then finalize the MSA redline).

Next steps requested from Redwood by EOD 11/14:
- Confirm which option Redwood will accept and provide final redline or mark-up.
- If accepting standalone $5M, provide the commercial concession you want us to review (Karthik's uplift or alternate).

I'll hold off on presenting to procurement until I hear back.

Julia
Wells & Co. LLP



--Forwarded message attached: redline_summary_20251112.txt (summary of proposed MSA/Order Form edits)

(End of thread)

title: Clarifying assets in scope for upcoming penetration test
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Paul Reynolds <paul.reynolds@acme.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 8 Nov 2025 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Clarifying assets in scope for upcoming penetration test

Hi Paul,

Thanks for the heads up on the SOC2 evidence packet — Acme's security team asked for the latest penetration test artifacts and a clear mapping of exactly which Redwood-managed components are included for each hosting variant (Hosted, Dedicated, Private/VPC, and on-prem package). Before we pull artifacts I wanted to verify a couple of boundaries so we only send relevant files:

1) For Dedicated deployments (customer-dedicated GPU pools): are the orchestration/control-plane endpoints (scheduler, orchestration API) considered in-scope, or do you only need the data-plane (inference API endpoints, request logs) and the tenant data stores?
2) For Private/VPC installs (customer-provisioned VPC with Redwood control plane): does Acme expect Redwood's control plane telemetry/artifacts if the customer manages the VPC networking? If so we need to know the exact artifact list you want (audit logs, helm manifests, orchestration config).
3) For the on-prem package we ship to regulated customers: we assume customer-managed infra (OS, network, KMS) is out-of-scope for Redwood's pen test. Please confirm — do you want evidence of our installer/packaging security and the release verified binary checksums instead?
4) KMS boundary: is customer-managed KMS always out-of-scope? For Hosted deployments we use Redwood-managed KMS for some metadata — does Acme want KMS audit trails from our side?

I've attached a lightweight inventory CSV that maps component names to the hosting variants (inventory_manifest_redwood.csv). If you can confirm the bullets above I will assemble the appropriate artifacts and the proposed boundary matrix for your review.

Best,

Ben Carter
Senior Manager, Security & Compliance
Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0143


From: Paul Reynolds <paul.reynolds@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Nov 2025 11:08:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Clarifying assets in scope for upcoming penetration test

Ben —

Thanks for the quick note and the inventory. A few clarifications so we can finalize Acme's SOC2 evidence bundle: we need a matrix that maps the following artifact types to hosting modes: network ingress/egress diagrams, access control lists (IAM roles), admin console exposure (which endpoints are reachable from the internet), KMS usage and audit trails, and the pen-test report itself with remediation statuses.

Specifically:
- For Dedicated: include data-plane endpoints, orchestration endpoints if they are reachable by Redwood ops, and any orchestration credentials (rotated or read-only tokens) used during normal ops.
- For Private/VPC: if Redwood does not have network access into the VPC, control-plane artifacts are optional; we will accept a signed statement plus exported audit logs if available.
- For on-prem: we expect packaging/security-of-distribution evidence (checksums, SBOM) plus our own test results (we may perform a follow-up).

Also please provide a proposed date by which Redwood can supply these artifacts and a remediation verification window for any findings. We're aiming to close the evidence request by end of month.

I've attached Acme_requirements.docx with our template for the artifact matrix.

Paul Reynolds
Security Engineer, Acme Corp
paul.reynolds@acme.com


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Cc: Paul Reynolds <paul.reynolds@acme.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Nov 2025 08:55:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Clarifying assets in scope for upcoming penetration test

Forwarding Acme's follow-up (below). Paul has outlined the artifact types they want mapped to hosting variants and asked for a delivery date + remediation verification window.

Quick internal notes:
- My read: we should avoid sending internal admin credentials or playbooks that contain operator-only secrets. We can provide sanitized orchestration endpoints, network diagrams, role mappings, and audit logs (redacted where appropriate).
- Proposed stance to present to Acme:
  * In-scope (for Hosted and Dedicated): inference API data-plane, orchestrator endpoints that Redwood connects to for telemetry/ops, rotated service tokens used by Redwood ops (read-only copies where possible), KMS audit logs for Redwood-managed keys.
  * Private/VPC: default out-of-scope for control plane if Redwood is not on the network; include control-plane artifacts only if customer explicitly enables audit extraction or grants read-only access.
  * On-prem bundle: include SBOM, installer checksums, and packaging hardening notes; do not include customer infra artifacts.

I'm attaching a quick matrix draft (proposed_boundary_matrix.xlsx) showing the mapping; can you review and add any required additional artifact names (e.g., exact filenames of audit exports, log slices, or evidence formats) before I send back to Acme?

On timing: can we commit to a first bundle by 2025-11-20 and remediation verification turnaround of two weeks after we receive Acme's review of the initial findings? If that feels tight, suggest alternatives.

Ben

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Cc: Paul Reynolds <paul.reynolds@acme.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Nov 2025 16:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Clarifying assets in scope for upcoming penetration test — agreed boundaries & next steps

Ben, Steph —

Thanks — below is a concise, evidence-friendly breakdown we can send to Acme. I also attached a control_plane_artifact_list.pdf with the exact log/export names we can produce.

Agreed boundary summary (to share with Acme):
- Hosted (Redwood-managed): IN-SCOPE
  * Data-plane: inference API endpoints, request traces, request/response logs (redacted PII), and tenant-level DB snapshots for repro steps (timeboxed).
  * Control-plane: orchestration services, scheduling API endpoints, and operator audit logs. KMS usage: Redwood-managed keys — we will export audit trails.

- Dedicated (customer-dedicated GPU pools with Redwood orchestration): PARTIAL IN-SCOPE
  * Data-plane: IN-SCOPE (same as Hosted).
  * Control-plane: IN-SCOPE for components that Redwood operates or accesses remotely (e.g., orchestration API endpoints reachable by Redwood ops). If a customer has private networking preventing Redwood access, control-plane artifacts will be provided only if customer grants read-only access or exports logs.

- Private/VPC (customer VPC with Redwood control plane optional): PARTIAL / OPTIONAL
  * If Redwood has no network path into the VPC, control-plane artifacts are OUT-OF-SCOPE by default.
  * We will provide a signed attestation describing the control plane responsibilities plus any exported audit logs the customer elects to share.

- On-prem package (air-gapped or customer-hosted installer): OUT-OF-SCOPE for customer infra; IN-SCOPE for Redwood deliverables (installer binary checksums, SBOM, packaging hardening notes, and integration test output).

Remediation & timeline:
- Proposed initial artifact package delivery: 2025-11-20 (inventory, network diagrams, role mappings, pen-test report excerpt if available).
- If findings are identified, we propose a 14-calendar-day remediation verification window for Redwood-managed items. For customer-managed items (on-prem, customer KMS) we will coordinate with Acme on remediation ownership and timelines.

Next steps:
1) Ben: send the attached proposed_boundary_matrix.xlsx updated with the exact filenames/formats Acme requested.
2) Steph: coordinate with Runtime team to export the following items by 11/18: orchestration audit log slice (last 90 days), KMS audit export (hosted keys), and network diagram exports.
3) Ben: deliver to Acme on 11/20 and set up a 30-min clarifying call (CC'd here) if they want a walkthrough.

If Acme agrees to the above, we should be comfortable providing the pen-test report and associated artifacts (RedTeam Labs report attached when available) while redacting operator secrets.

Lauren Bishop
Director, Product Security
Redwood Inference
lauren@redwood.com

title: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)
preview:
From: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 8:42 AM
Subject: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)
Attachments: AsterBank_Redwood_Security_Questionnaire_v3.7.xlsx

Hi Dr. Rahman,

As discussed in last week’s security review, attaching our questionnaire (v3.7). We’re specifically looking for detailed responses for the sections on:

- Supply chain / artifact integrity (signing and verification)
- Offline / air-gapped installation and upgrades
- Key custody, rotation, and revocation
- Evidence we can retain internally for auditors (who/what/when was signed)
- Audit logging produced by the product when verification succeeds/fails

We need responses by EOD Wednesday to stay on track for our December change window.

Thanks,
Priya Nair
Director, Security Assurance
AsterBank


---

From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>
Cc: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)
Attachments: Redwood_Private_Offline_Bundles_Signing_Overview_2025-11-10.pdf, Redwood_Artifact_Signing_Standard_v1.0.pdf, Redwood_SOC2_TypeII_2025.pdf

Priya,

Thank you — we can meet your Wednesday deadline.

For speed, below are direct answers to the artifact signing / air-gap items while Connor and Marcus fill the spreadsheet sections (we’ll return the completed XLSX).

1) What is signed for offline/air-gapped installs?
- Our offline bundle includes the installer, deployment manifests/Helm charts, and a manifest enumerating bundle contents with cryptographic hashes.
- The signed object is the canonicalized bundle manifest (covers: versions/build identifiers, component list, SHA-256 digests for files included, and pointers to SBOM artifacts when provided).
- Verification is performed fully offline. The installer/preflight blocks install/upgrade if signature verification fails or if bundle contents do not match the signed manifest.

2) Signature format and algorithm
- We use a detached signature (e.g., manifest + manifest.sig) over a canonical representation of the manifest.
- Signing keys are based on modern asymmetric cryptography (Ed25519 in our current implementation) and signatures are verified by the on-prem installer.

3) Key custody / HSM / access controls
- Release signing keys are generated and stored in a dedicated key management environment with strict separation of duties.
- Access is limited to a small Release Engineering operator group with approvals and audited actions. Human access to private key material is restricted; key usage is mediated via controlled signing operations.
- The key custody process and approvals are auditable (we can provide a written description suitable for your vendor risk file; see attached overview).

4) Rotation and revocation
- We support a trust store model in the installer so multiple signing public keys can be trusted concurrently (to allow a smooth rotation window).
- We publish new public keys and provide a documented process for customers to update trusted roots in air-gapped environments.
- If a signing key is suspected compromised, our emergency response includes revocation, rotation, and a “fail closed” verification posture (new releases are signed only by the replacement key; customers update trust store prior to applying the update).

5) Downgrade/replay protection
- The signed manifest includes bundle version/build metadata. The installer maintains local install state to prevent accidental or malicious downgrade to older versions where policy requires it.

6) Audit logging / evidence
- The installer emits local audit events for bundle verification outcomes (success/failure + reason codes). These can be retained locally and, where configured, exported to your SIEM pipeline.
- We can provide: (a) signing policy/standard document, (b) a summary of the key ceremony/controls, and (c) release metadata that correlates a running deployment to a specific signed bundle build ID.

Connor will send the completed questionnaire file back in a single response, and we can schedule a 30-minute follow-up to walk through any items your auditors flag.

Regards,
Aisha Rahman, PhD
VP Security & Compliance
Redwood Inference


---

From: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 11:21 AM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)

Thanks Aisha — helpful.

Two follow-ups we need to answer internally:

1) Time validity: Do signatures require accurate time on the target host? Our air-gapped zones can have clock drift.
2) Customer-controlled trust root: Can AsterBank provide our own signing key/cert and have bundles signed by us instead of Redwood?

If the answer to #2 is “not today,” please confirm whether we can pin Redwood keys and get notified of rotations well in advance.

Tom


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>
Cc: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 1:07 PM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)

Tom,

On the two follow-ups:

1) Time validity / clock drift
- Our verification does not depend on contacting an online time source.
- The signature verification itself does not require accurate system time (cryptographic verification is time-independent).
- Where time can matter is if you choose to enforce certificate validity windows (e.g., X.509 chains). For the offline bundle signing path we’re using a key-based trust store rather than online PKI validation. Net: moderate clock drift in air-gapped environments is not expected to break verification.
- Practically, we still recommend a reliable internal time source for general operational hygiene; we’ll include a short “time skew” troubleshooting note in the install guide.

2) Customer-controlled trust root / customer-signed bundles
- Not in the initial phase. The current design is “Redwood-signed bundles, customer verifies offline.”
- We do support pinning to a set of Redwood public keys (trust store) and we support overlapping keys for rotations.
- If you have a hard policy requirement for customer-signed bundles, we can document it as a roadmap item and propose an interim control (e.g., you verify Redwood signatures + you separately hash/attest bundles after receipt in your internal artifact system).

Connor will also include the rotation notice expectations in the XLSX response.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Security Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


---

To: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>
Cc: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 11, 2025 at 9:34 AM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)
Attachments: AsterBank_Redwood_Security_Questionnaire_v3.7_COMPLETED_Redwood_2025-11-11.xlsx

Priya,

Attached is the completed questionnaire (v3.7) with the requested details in the “Supply Chain,” “Deployment Model,” and “Audit/Logging” tabs.

Highlights you may want to copy into your internal controls mapping:
- Bundles are verified offline; installer fails closed on signature failure or hash mismatch.
- Signing occurs in our release pipeline with restricted operator approvals; signing events are logged and retained per our security logging policy.
- Installer produces a local audit record for verification result + reason code, and includes bundle build ID to support change management evidence.
- Key rotation: we maintain overlapping trust roots during rotation windows; customers can pin trust to a published set of Redwood public keys. For air-gapped customers, we provide updated trust store artifacts via your approved secure transfer method.

If you have a specific notice period requirement (e.g., 60/90 days) for key rotation communications, please send it and we’ll align on an SLA for your account.

Regards,
Connor O’Brien
Release Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference

Date: Tue, Nov 11, 2025 at 4:18 PM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)

Priya,

Adding one note for your auditors: our default posture for offline bundle verification is strict (“fail closed”). If there is ever a break-glass requirement during an incident (e.g., verification cannot be performed due to environmental tooling failure), we treat that as an exception with explicit approvals and local audit markings.

We can walk you through that exception process live if it’s relevant to your change control board, but the expectation is that normal installs/upgrades do not rely on break-glass.

If helpful, propose 30 minutes Thursday 10:00–10:30am PT or Friday 1:00–1:30pm PT.

Aisha Rahman, PhD

Redwood Inference
text:
From: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 8:42 AM
Subject: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)
Attachments: AsterBank_Redwood_Security_Questionnaire_v3.7.xlsx

Hi Dr. Rahman,

As discussed in last week’s security review, attaching our questionnaire (v3.7). We’re specifically looking for detailed responses for the sections on:

- Supply chain / artifact integrity (signing and verification)
- Offline / air-gapped installation and upgrades
- Key custody, rotation, and revocation
- Evidence we can retain internally for auditors (who/what/when was signed)
- Audit logging produced by the product when verification succeeds/fails

We need responses by EOD Wednesday to stay on track for our December change window.

Thanks,
Priya Nair
Director, Security Assurance
AsterBank


---

From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>
Cc: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)
Attachments: Redwood_Private_Offline_Bundles_Signing_Overview_2025-11-10.pdf, Redwood_Artifact_Signing_Standard_v1.0.pdf, Redwood_SOC2_TypeII_2025.pdf

Priya,

Thank you — we can meet your Wednesday deadline.

For speed, below are direct answers to the artifact signing / air-gap items while Connor and Marcus fill the spreadsheet sections (we’ll return the completed XLSX).

1) What is signed for offline/air-gapped installs?
- Our offline bundle includes the installer, deployment manifests/Helm charts, and a manifest enumerating bundle contents with cryptographic hashes.
- The signed object is the canonicalized bundle manifest (covers: versions/build identifiers, component list, SHA-256 digests for files included, and pointers to SBOM artifacts when provided).
- Verification is performed fully offline. The installer/preflight blocks install/upgrade if signature verification fails or if bundle contents do not match the signed manifest.

2) Signature format and algorithm
- We use a detached signature (e.g., manifest + manifest.sig) over a canonical representation of the manifest.
- Signing keys are based on modern asymmetric cryptography (Ed25519 in our current implementation) and signatures are verified by the on-prem installer.

3) Key custody / HSM / access controls
- Release signing keys are generated and stored in a dedicated key management environment with strict separation of duties.
- Access is limited to a small Release Engineering operator group with approvals and audited actions. Human access to private key material is restricted; key usage is mediated via controlled signing operations.
- The key custody process and approvals are auditable (we can provide a written description suitable for your vendor risk file; see attached overview).

4) Rotation and revocation
- We support a trust store model in the installer so multiple signing public keys can be trusted concurrently (to allow a smooth rotation window).
- We publish new public keys and provide a documented process for customers to update trusted roots in air-gapped environments.
- If a signing key is suspected compromised, our emergency response includes revocation, rotation, and a “fail closed” verification posture (new releases are signed only by the replacement key; customers update trust store prior to applying the update).

5) Downgrade/replay protection
- The signed manifest includes bundle version/build metadata. The installer maintains local install state to prevent accidental or malicious downgrade to older versions where policy requires it.

6) Audit logging / evidence
- The installer emits local audit events for bundle verification outcomes (success/failure + reason codes). These can be retained locally and, where configured, exported to your SIEM pipeline.
- We can provide: (a) signing policy/standard document, (b) a summary of the key ceremony/controls, and (c) release metadata that correlates a running deployment to a specific signed bundle build ID.

Connor will send the completed questionnaire file back in a single response, and we can schedule a 30-minute follow-up to walk through any items your auditors flag.

Regards,
Aisha Rahman, PhD
VP Security & Compliance
Redwood Inference


---

From: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 11:21 AM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)

Thanks Aisha — helpful.

Two follow-ups we need to answer internally:

1) Time validity: Do signatures require accurate time on the target host? Our air-gapped zones can have clock drift.
2) Customer-controlled trust root: Can AsterBank provide our own signing key/cert and have bundles signed by us instead of Redwood?

If the answer to #2 is “not today,” please confirm whether we can pin Redwood keys and get notified of rotations well in advance.

Tom


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Tom Jensen <tom.jensen@asterbank.com>
Cc: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Nair <priya.nair@asterbank.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, security-questionnaires@asterbank.com
Date: Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 1:07 PM
Subject: Re: AsterBank security questionnaire - artifact signing, offline installs, and audit evidence (Redwood Private)

Tom,

On the two follow-ups:

1) Time validity / clock drift
- Our verification does not depend on contacting an online time source.
- The signature verification itself does not require accurate system time (cryptographic verification is time-independent).
- Where time can matter is if you choose to enforce certificate validity windows (e.g., X.509 chains). For the offline bundle signing path we’re using a key-based trust store rather than online PKI validation. Net: moderate clock drift in air-gapped environments is not expected to break verification.
- Practically, we still recommend a reliable internal time source for general operational hygiene; we’ll include a short “time skew” troubleshooting note in the install guide.

2) Customer-controlled trust root / customer-signed bundles
- Not in the initial phase. The current design is “Redwood-signed bundles, customer verifies offline.”
- We do support pinning to a set of Redwood public keys (trust store) and we support overlapping keys for rotations.
- If you have a hard policy requirement for customer-signed bundles, we can document it as a roadmap item and propose an interim control (e.g., you verify Redwood signatures + you separately hash/attest bundles after receipt in your internal artifact system).

Connor will also include the rotation notice expectations in the XLSX response.

Marcus

Redwood Inference

title: Atlas MAP: milestone-tied pricing proposal and ramp triggers
preview:
From: Emily Zhang <emily@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@atlashealth.com>
Date: 2025-11-03T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: MAP & pricing — evaluation milestones and discounting

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. We liked the latency and the controls for data residency. Our procurement asked for a clear Mutual Action Plan (MAP) so they can see when discounts kick in and when committed capacity will start billing. Key asks: a checklist of evaluation success criteria, milestone dates, and explicit pricing/discount triggers tied to those milestones.

Can you send a MAP that shows:
- POC success criteria (3 items)
- 30/60/90 day ramp plan with expected RPS and token volumes
- Conditional discounts if we hit checkpoint metrics (POC pass, production ramp > X tokens)

If we can get that by end of week, I can feed to procurement for an initial go/no-go.

Thanks,
Emily
Account Lead, Atlas Health

Attachments: none


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-11-03T13:45:00-08:00
Subject: RE: MAP & pricing — evaluation milestones and discounting

Thanks Emily — attaching a draft MAP and a conditional pricing table for review. High level: we propose three checkpoints (POC sign-off, 30-day production pilot, 90-day committed ramp). Discounts are milestone-conditioned and cumulative (i.e., once a milestone is met, the corresponding discount applies going forward).

Highlights:
- POC: success = model accuracy on your eval set + latency < 300ms p95 + integration completion
- Pilot (30d): target 50k tokens/day average; unlocked 10% discount on generation unit price
- Ramp (90d): target 250k tokens/day average sustained for 14 days; additional 15% discount and option to convert to a committed reserved pool with a 12-month commit for deeper pricing

I've left some cells for Atlas-specific volumes — happy to walk through tomorrow.

Ben
Senior Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
Attachments: MAP_Atlas_v2.xlsx (spreadsheet, 48KB)


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T08:07:00-08:00
Subject: Internal: Atlas MAP — discount thresholds

Ben / Dylan — quick alignment before we send a refined doc back to Emily. A few points:

1) The proposed 25% total discount at Ramp (10% + 15%) is fine but we need sign-off from Finance to offer the 15% ramp bonus absent a committed ARR conversion. Can we convert that 15% into an "option" that is contingent on them electing a 12-month reserved pool within 30 days of hitting ramp?

2) Add a hard cap for early-stage pilot overage pricing (so they aren't surprised if traffic spikes).

3) We should include an evaluation credit (one-time) to cover tooling/ingestion costs — $5k — to smooth procurement conversations.

If you both agree, I'll loop in Finance and Legal for a quick approval path.

Laura
Director, Commercial Sales Operations


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T09:30:00-08:00
Subject: RE: Internal: Atlas MAP — discount thresholds

Laura — agree on all counts. Proposal changes I'll make:
- Make the 15% ramp bonus conditional on electing the 12-month reserved pool within 30 days of hitting the 90-day ramp; if they don't elect, bonus is reduced to 5%.
- Add a pilot overage rate schedule and an overage notice window (48 hours) before auto-scaling.
- Insert a line-item: $5,000 evaluation credit to be applied to first invoice.

I'll update MAP_Atlas_v2.xlsx and send to Emily with a short note calling out the conditional language. Dylan — can you flag any procurement objections you foresee so we can preempt them in the doc?

Ben


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-11-05T11:02:00-08:00

Emily / Tom — attaching an updated MAP and a short one-page summary that highlights the conditional discount language and next steps. Key changes from the draft:

- Ramp bonus (15%) now requires conversion to a 12-month reserved pool within 30 days of meeting the 90-day ramp threshold; if Atlas opts not to convert, an on-demand fallback discount of 5% will remain.
- Added $5,000 evaluation credit to be applied against the first invoice.
- Pilot overage schedule and 48-hour notice window added.

Please review and let me know if procurement needs any alternative wording. Happy to jump on a 30-min call to walk this through.

Best,
Ben
Attachments: MAP_Atlas_v2.xlsx (updated), summary_pricing_conditions.txt


From: Emily Zhang <emily@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@atlashealth.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.com>

2) Please include the final quoted monthly run-rate at the three checkpoints assuming Atlas hits targets (we filled your volume cells and attached them).

If you can send those two items by Monday, we can move to an internal approval meeting and aim for signature by mid-November.

title: Reserved commit sizing: throughput guarantees vs burst credits for HorizonBio
preview:
From: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: HorizonBio Procurement <procurement@horizonbio.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T09:13:00-08:00
Subject: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Alex,

Thanks for taking this. Quick background: HorizonBio is evaluating Redwood Dedicated for our clinical-genomics assistant. We expect long contexts (mostly 8k tokens), mixed generation (summaries + structured outputs), and a variable request pattern. Here's our rough profile: 
- Average requests: 60/min sustained
- Peak requests: 220/min for 6–12 minute windows (concurrent users ~40–60)
- Avg tokens per request (input+output): ~3k
- Latency target: p95 < 1.5s, p99 < 2.5s

We need guidance on:
1) Recommended GPU pool (type and #) for a reserved commit that meets the latency targets for sustained and peak traffic
2) How burst credits or a burst pool would work with a reserved commit (tradeoffs, costs)
3) How context length / KV cache affects sizing and whether caching reduces commit needs
4) What discounts you can offer for a 12-month reserved commit vs a 6-month

Please attach any workload sizing template or quick TCO sketch. Procurement timeline: aim to finalize sizing by Nov 14 so we can move to contract review.

Emily
Emily Zhou
VP Product, HorizonBio
---
Attachment: horizonbio_workload_profile.xlsx (spreadsheet stub)

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Emily — thanks for the clear profile. High level answers up front, with a short sizing rationale below; attaching a lightweight workbook to show assumptions.

Short recommendation (initial commit):
- Baseline: 3 x NVIDIA H100-80GB (sustained pool) + 1 x H100 reserved for burst (hot spare) — effective pool = 4 but committed-to baseline = 3
- Expected sustained throughput: ~75–100 req/min at p95 < 1.2s for 8k contexts (depending on model variant). The burst spare covers short 6–12 minute peaks up to ~220 req/min when combined with autoscale and request queuing.
- If you need p95 < 0.7s for sustained traffic, move to 4x committed H100; that covers higher concurrency without relying on burst.

Sizing rationale / notes:
- Long context (8k) increases per-request compute and KV cache pressure; sequential attention scales roughly O(seq^2) for some kernels but our runtime uses chunked attention optimizations for long-context models — still, throughput drops ~35–50% vs 2k contexts.
- KV cache helps when many requests share recent prefixes; for HorizonBio workloads we saw limited cross-request prefix reuse, so we model only modest cache benefit (~10–15% effective throughput uplift).
- Batching: continuous batching with a 20–40ms aggregation window helps throughput without materially affecting p95 under moderate concurrency.
- Quantized model variants: recommend a 4-bit quantized medium-quality variant for non-clinical endpoints — ~25–35% throughput gain vs full-precision, with small quality delta. For clinical inference keep fp16 or bf16.

Commit / pricing guidance:
- 12-month reserved commit (3x H100 baseline): estimated list discount 15% — final depends on committed monthly hours / token floors.
- 6-month: ~10% discount.
- We can offer an 18% effective discount for 12-month if you include a minimum monthly token floor (we'll sketch numbers in the workbook).

Attachments: workload-sizing-summary.csv (assumptions + per-GPU throughput table).

Happy to walk through numbers—I can propose a formal sizing doc + SLA draft if this aligns.

—Alex
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, HorizonBio Procurement <procurement@horizonbio.com>
Date: 2025-11-05T08:50:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Alex — this is helpful. A few follow-ups: 
- Can you share a cost estimate (monthly $) for the 3x-committed + 1 burst spare config vs 4x-committed? Put baseline and peak columns.
- For the 18% discount path you mentioned, what is the required monthly token floor (rough estimate)?
- Are quantized variants something we can toggle per-route so we can keep clinical endpoints at fp16 but non-clinical at 4-bit?
- We will need SOC2 evidence and a short SLA for uptime and latency before procurement signs off.

Also looping Procurement — we'll target Nov 14 for sizing sign-off but we may push to Nov 21 if SOC2 review takes longer.

Thanks,
Emily

Option A — Cost-conscious hybrid (recommended initial pilot)

Option B — Performance-first (slightly higher commit)
- Commit: 4 x H100-80GB (no metered burst)
- Sustained capacity: ~150 req/min at p95 < 0.9s for 8k contexts (fp16)
- Peak handling: headroom for 220–250 req/min with p95 under 1.0s; more predictable latency SLOs
- Estimated cost (net of 15% list discount): $80,500/month

- If you commit to a 12-month term with a 120M token monthly floor, we can do 18% off list and include 1 month of free burst credits at signing (applied to the first invoice).
- A 6-month term we typically price at ~10% discount and cannot include the free burst credits.

Technical ops notes: 
- KV cache: for HorizonBio payloads we recommend enabling per-session KV caching with TTL=20m for multi-step interactions. That yields a modest throughput uplift but primarily reduces cost for workflows with repeated prefixes.
- Autoscaling: we recommend a conservative autoscaling policy for the first 4 weeks (scale step 0.5 GPU, cooldown 90s) to avoid rapid oscillation.
- Model mix: keep clinical endpoints on fp16/bf16; route non-clinical interactive UX to a quantized 4-bit model behind a separate route to save cost. This routing is per-endpoint and available in config.

Subject: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Alex,

Thanks for taking this. Quick background: HorizonBio is evaluating Redwood Dedicated for our clinical-genomics assistant. We expect long contexts (mostly 8k tokens), mixed generation (summaries + structured outputs), and a variable request pattern. Here's our rough profile: 
- Average requests: 60/min sustained
- Peak requests: 220/min for 6–12 minute windows (concurrent users ~40–60)
- Avg tokens per request (input+output): ~3k
- Latency target: p95 < 1.5s, p99 < 2.5s

We need guidance on:
1) Recommended GPU pool (type and #) for a reserved commit that meets the latency targets for sustained and peak traffic
2) How burst credits or a burst pool would work with a reserved commit (tradeoffs, costs)
3) How context length / KV cache affects sizing and whether caching reduces commit needs
4) What discounts you can offer for a 12-month reserved commit vs a 6-month

Please attach any workload sizing template or quick TCO sketch. Procurement timeline: aim to finalize sizing by Nov 14 so we can move to contract review.

Emily
Emily Zhou
VP Product, HorizonBio

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Emily — thanks for the clear profile. High level answers up front, with a short sizing rationale below; attaching a lightweight workbook to show assumptions.

Short recommendation (initial commit):
- Baseline: 3 x NVIDIA H100-80GB (sustained pool) + 1 x H100 reserved for burst (hot spare) — effective pool = 4 but committed-to baseline = 3
- Expected sustained throughput: ~75–100 req/min at p95 < 1.2s for 8k contexts (depending on model variant). The burst spare covers short 6–12 minute peaks up to ~220 req/min when combined with autoscale and request queuing.
- If you need p95 < 0.7s for sustained traffic, move to 4x committed H100; that covers higher concurrency without relying on burst.

Sizing rationale / notes:
- Long context (8k) increases per-request compute and KV cache pressure; sequential attention scales roughly O(seq^2) for some kernels but our runtime uses chunked attention optimizations for long-context models — still, throughput drops ~35–50% vs 2k contexts.
- KV cache helps when many requests share recent prefixes; for HorizonBio workloads we saw limited cross-request prefix reuse, so we model only modest cache benefit (~10–15% effective throughput uplift).
- Batching: continuous batching with a 20–40ms aggregation window helps throughput without materially affecting p95 under moderate concurrency.
- Quantized model variants: recommend a 4-bit quantized medium-quality variant for non-clinical endpoints — ~25–35% throughput gain vs full-precision, with small quality delta. For clinical inference keep fp16 or bf16.

Commit / pricing guidance:
- 12-month reserved commit (3x H100 baseline): estimated list discount 15% — final depends on committed monthly hours / token floors.
- 6-month: ~10% discount.
- We can offer an 18% effective discount for 12-month if you include a minimum monthly token floor (we'll sketch numbers in the workbook).

Attachments: workload-sizing-summary.csv (assumptions + per-GPU throughput table).

Happy to walk through numbers—I can propose a formal sizing doc + SLA draft if this aligns.

—Alex
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

To: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 4:18 PM UTC
Subject: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Hi Ava,

Were seeing our streaming chat responses stall mid-output. The HTTP connection stays open but tokens stop coming. We dont receive a terminal done event, so the UI looks like its hanging.

Example request IDs (prod us-east):
- rwreq_9b2f0c8c (2025-11-07 16:02 UTC)
- rwreq_1a77d3e1 (2025-11-07 15:57 UTC)

Can you advise?

Thanks,
Nina

---

To: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 5:01 PM UTC
Subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Thanks Nina  were investigating and looping in SRE + runtime engineering. Well update shortly.

---

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>

title: Annual retainer invoicing cadence & drawdown ledger — Starling Health
preview:
From: Marta Ruiz <accounts-payable@starlinghealth.com>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 1 Nov 2025 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Question on retainer/invoice cadence for 2026 retainer

Hi Vanessa,

Quick one from AP as we finalize Opex for next year — we received your November invoice and the SOW shows a single annual retainer that should be drawn down monthly from our prepay balance. We want to align the ledger on our side so we can automate the drawdowns. Two quick asks: 1) can you confirm the monthly drawdown amount you expect against the invoice (i.e., how you’ll amortize the retainer), and 2) confirm whether Redwood will issue monthly statements or a single statement with a monthly ledger breakdown?

Also, for VAT mapping: do you need our VAT registration number on the invoice or just the PO? Our tax team flagged that some EU cross-border rules might affect reporting if we treat this as a single prepay vs. monthly bill.

Thanks,
Marta
Accounts Payable
Starling Health

Attachment: PO_Starling_2025-11.pdf (pdf)


From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
To: Marta Ruiz <accounts-payable@starlinghealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Question on retainer/invoice cadence for 2026 retainer

Hi Marta — thanks for the note and the PO. Short answers first: we amortize the annual retainer evenly across the twelve months unless you request a custom ladder; we can provide a monthly ledger/statement that shows the starting prepay balance, each draw, and remaining balance. VAT: please include your VAT registration number on the PO and we will mirror it on the invoice.

I’ve attached the proposed schedule and an example month-by-month ledger so your automation rules can map fields directly. If you prefer a fixed monthly invoice (e.g., 12 x fixed amounts) rather than one annual invoice with ledger lines, we can do that as well — it just changes the invoice document set we issue.

Proposed monthly drawdown: $41,666.67 (annual retainer $500,000).

Next steps: 1) confirm preferred invoice document type (single annual + ledger vs. 12 monthly invoices), 2) confirm VAT number to include, and 3) if you want any custom GL/ledger fields added to the PDF.

Cheers,
Vanessa Ortiz
Enterprise Sales 
Redwood Inference
Tel: +1 415 555 0198

Attachments: Starling_Retainer_Schedule_2026.pdf (pdf), Redwood_Invoice_2025-11-01.pdf (pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 Nov 2025 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Question on retainer/invoice cadence for 2026 retainer

Looping in Marissa to confirm operational treatment. A few notes from finance: if we issue a single annual invoice with a ledger, revenue recognition and tax documentation are fine but AP teams sometimes prefer monthly invoices for their ERP reconciliation. Either approach is acceptable — we just need to record the drawdown entries the same way.

Marissa — can finance generate the example CSV that maps ledger lines to the invoice fields (date, description, debit/credit, remaining balance)? If we can attach that to the invoice PDF or deliver via SFTP, it will make Starling’s automation much easier.

Ben
Director, Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

Thanks for the context. We can support both delivery formats. Operational proposal:
- Option A (single invoice + monthly ledger): issue annual invoice dated Nov 1 for $500,000; attach a CSV ledger with 12 lines (01-Nov-2025 start balance = 500,000; first draw 01-Dec-2025 = 41,666.67; etc.). We will include Starling’s VAT registration on the invoice and flag the ledger CSV with the PO number for automated ingestion.
- Option B (12 monthly invoices): generate 12 sequential monthly invoices (Dec 2025 — Nov 2026) each for $41,666.67. Each invoice will reference the annual PO and include VAT where applicable.

We recommend Option A for lower document churn and clearer single-source-of-truth, but we will accommodate Option B if their ERP requires discrete invoices. Attached is the example CSV export and a sample ledger PDF. We can also publish the CSV to an SFTP location upon request.

Marissa Cole
Revenue Operations Manager
Redwood Inference

title: Upfront credit vs tranche invoicing — Novus commercial path
preview:
From: Maya Singh <maya@novus.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: James Liu <james.liu@novus.ai>, finance@novus.ai
Date: 2025-11-03T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Invoice cadence question — upfront credit vs tranches

Ben — thanks again for a useful demo yesterday. Finance pushed back on the Order Form as drafted: they want clarity on whether the committed credit is taken upfront (single invoice) or invoiced in three tranches tied to activation milestones.

Quick context: Novus prefers predictable cashflow but also needs monthly reconciliation with our internal cost centers. If we do upfront billing we can offset with a credit balance; if tranches, we need clear net terms per tranche, and VAT handling for EU jurisdictions.

Could you outline both options and any legal/ops caveats so we can fast-track internal approval? We have a Monday board review and would like to finalize commercial approach this week.

Thanks,
Maya
Director, Strategic Partnerships
Novus Analytics

--
Attachment: Novus_Questions_v1.pdf (pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Singh <maya@novus.ai>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2025-11-03T11:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Invoice cadence question — upfront credit vs tranches

Maya — good question. High level options below; I attached a short spreadsheet with cashflow comparisons.

Option A — Upfront invoice (single invoice at signing)
- Pros: predictable cashflow for Redwood; fewer invoices to manage for both sides; immediate recognition for committed credit.
- Cons: Novus carries pre-paid balance on books; refunds/credits require a reversal process.
- Accounting/legal notes: Net terms are effectively immediate payment on receipt (we can offer Net 15 for large customers). Tax: treat as prepaid service; VAT/residency depends on invoice recipient country.

Option B — Tranche invoicing (3 equal tranches: kickoff, +90 days, +180 days)
- Pros: matches Novus internal cost centers; spreads cashflow for Novus.
- Cons: Administrative overhead; requires milestone definitions and a fallback if a tranche fails (e.g., if activation delayed).
- Accounting/legal notes: Each tranche treated as a separate taxable event; VAT may be due per tranche depending on delivery location. Net terms apply per invoice (we suggest Net 30).

Recommendation: Finance prefers tranches for customer retention and lower upfront buyer friction, but Redwood can be flexible if we secure an initial non-refundable activation fee (small %) to cover ramp. If Novus wants preferability on support or pricing laddering we could trade for a larger upfront portion.

Next steps I propose: 1) Confirm which option Novus prefers; 2) If tranches, define milestone acceptance language in the Order Form; 3) Kimberly to review legal redlines on net terms and tax clauses; 4) Karthik to confirm AR/process for split invoices.

Attached: payment-options.xlsx

-Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference

> On Nov 3, 2025, at 09:12, Maya Singh <maya@novus.ai> wrote:
> \"Ben — thanks again for a useful demo yesterday...\"


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Maya Singh <maya@novus.ai>
Date: 2025-11-04T08:47:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Invoice cadence question — upfront credit vs tranches

Folks — quick legal view:

1) Milestone definitions: If we do tranche invoicing we need precise acceptance/cutover language tied to objective criteria (e.g., model endpoint available, production traffic > X requests/day for 7 consecutive days) to avoid disputes. Vague language = billing delays and collection headaches.

2) Net terms & late fees: Sales can offer Net 30 on each tranche, but we should keep a clause that unpaid tranches > 60 days can trigger suspension of service and removal of future discounts. Legal will not accept open-ended grace without escalation.

3) Tax/VAT: For EU-resident customers, VAT is typically due at the point of supply per invoice. If Novus invoices across multiple entities, we need to know invoice recipient legal entity and VAT ID to apply reverse-charge where appropriate. Please get Novus to complete the tax evidence table (VAT ID, billing entity, invoice recipient).

4) Upfront credit mechanics: If sales prefers upfront billing with a credit balance, we need refund mechanics spelled out (non-refundable activation fee vs refundable prepayment) and currency revaluation clause for long-term credits.

I'll produce a redline for the Order Form with suggested milestone language and a short clause for the billing path. For now I recommend tranches + a 10% non-refundable activation fee to balance risk.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Compliance, Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: redlines_ks_changes.docx (docx)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Singh <maya@novus.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2025-11-05T15:08:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Invoice cadence question — upfront credit vs tranches

Maya — finance has reviewed. Short answer: we can support tranche invoicing with the following binding constraints: 

1) Payment schedule: three invoices at signing (activation), 90 days, 180 days. Net 30 on each invoice.
2) Activation fee: 10% non-refundable invoice at signing (applied to total commitment).
3) VAT handling: Novus to provide VAT ID and invoice recipient entity. If Novus uses a EU entity with valid VAT ID, we will apply reverse-charge where allowed; otherwise Redwood will charge VAT and Novus is responsible for reclaiming.
4) PO/Invoice routing: we need a PO number on the first invoice and an AP contact (name+email) for each tranche. Without PO the invoice will be delayed in our AR system.

If this is acceptable, Ben: please update the Order Form to: (a) include the 10% activation fee line, (b) include milestone acceptance language Kimberly will provide, and (c) add a simple table for VAT ID / invoice recipient. Maya: if you can share the VAT ID and AP contact by EOD Friday we can generate the first invoice and get this into your board packet.

Karthik
Director, Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

> On Nov 4, 2025, at 08:47, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai> wrote:
> \"Folks — quick legal view:...\"
